bio_short_en: Syrian scholar, judge, and prolific writer known for his literary style and pioneering Islamic television programs. His memoirs (Dhikrayat) and accessible books on Islam reached millions of readers across the Arab world.
bio_en:
Ali ibn Mustafa at-Tantawi (1909-1999 CE / 1327-1420 AH) was a Syrian scholar, judge, and prolific writer who became one of the most beloved Islamic literary figures of the Arab world. Born in Damascus into a family of scholars, he studied under leading Syrian scholars and was influenced by the writings of al-Manfaluti and Mustafa Sadiq ar-Rafi'i, developing an elegant Arabic prose style.

At-Tantawi served as a judge in the Syrian Shariah courts and later taught at the Islamic University of Madinah and at other Saudi institutions after leaving Syria. He pioneered Islamic programming on television and radio, becoming one of the first scholars to use modern media for Islamic education. His literary works include Dhikrayat (Memories), an eight-volume autobiography that is considered a masterpiece of modern Arabic prose; Qisas min at-Tarikh (Stories from History), engaging retellings of Islamic historical episodes; and Ta'rif Amm bi-Din al-Islam (A General Introduction to Islam), a widely read introduction to Islamic beliefs and practices.

At-Tantawi's gift was his ability to present Islamic knowledge in beautiful, accessible prose that engaged readers emotionally and intellectually. He wrote for newspapers and magazines throughout his career, reaching a broad audience beyond the traditional scholarly community. His approach combined scholarly substance with literary grace, and his works continue to be widely read across the Arab world. He passed away in Jeddah, Saudi Arabia.

title: Sheikh
bio_short_en: Grand Mufti of Saudi Arabia, a major Hanbali scholar known for his fatwas, teaching, and charitable works.
bio_en:
Abdul-Aziz ibn Abdullah ibn Abdul-Rahman ibn Baz (1912-1999 CE / 1330-1420 AH) was the Grand Mufti of Saudi Arabia and one of the most influential Sunni scholars of the 20th century. Born in Riyadh, he studied under the leading scholars of the city, including Muhammad ibn Ibrahim Al ash-Sheikh. He lost his eyesight in his early twenties due to illness but continued his scholarly pursuits with remarkable determination, relying on his exceptional memory.

Ibn Baz served in numerous official religious positions throughout his career: judge in al-Kharj, head of the Islamic University of Madinah, head of the Permanent Committee for Islamic Research and Ifta, and finally Grand Mufti of Saudi Arabia from 1993 until his death. His fatawa, collected in Majmu Fatawa wa-Maqalat Mutanawwiah (spanning over thirty volumes), cover virtually every aspect of Islamic law and practice. He was known for his accessibility, with people from all walks of life consulting him on religious matters. He also authored important works on creed including Sharh Kitab at-Tawhid and wrote extensively on contemporary issues.

Ibn Baz was known for his humility, generosity, and devotion to teaching and issuing fatawa. He championed the cause of Islamic education and charitable work, establishing and supporting countless institutions. His legal opinions shaped religious practice across Saudi Arabia and influenced Muslims worldwide through his radio programs, published works, and the scholars he trained. Despite his blindness, his prodigious memory and dedication made him one of the most productive and accessible scholars of his era. He passed away in Riyadh and millions attended his funeral prayer.

title: Sheikh
bio_short_en: One of the foremost hadith scholars of the modern era, spending decades grading thousands of hadith.
bio_en:
Muhammad Nasir ad-Din ibn Nuh Najati al-Albani (1914-1999 CE / 1332-1420 AH) was one of the most influential hadith scholars of the modern era. Born in Shkodra, Albania, his family emigrated to Damascus when he was a child. He learned watchmaking from his father and was largely self-taught in the Islamic sciences, spending years studying hadith manuscripts in the Zahiriyyah Library of Damascus, where he developed his extraordinary expertise in hadith authentication.

Al-Albani's scholarly output was immense and transformative. His most important works include Silsilat al-Ahadith as-Sahihah (Series of Authentic Hadith) and Silsilat al-Ahadith ad-Da'ifah (Series of Weak Hadith), which together comprise thousands of hadith with detailed authentication. He also produced authenticated editions of the major hadith collections: Sahih and Da'if Sunan Abi Dawud, Sahih and Da'if Sunan at-Tirmidhi, Sahih and Da'if Sunan an-Nasa'i, Sahih and Da'if Sunan Ibn Majah, and Sahih al-Jami as-Saghir. His Irwa al-Ghalil verified the hadith evidence behind Hanbali fiqh rulings, and Sifat Salat an-Nabi described the Prophet's prayer based on authenticated evidence.

Al-Albani spent periods teaching at the Islamic University of Madinah and later settled in Amman, Jordan, where he continued his research until his death. He was awarded the King Faisal International Prize for Islamic Studies in 1999. His methodology of evaluating hadith according to their chain of narration, independent of the opinions of any legal school, revitalized the science of hadith criticism in the modern era. His gradings are widely referenced by scholars and students worldwide, and he is considered alongside Ibn Baz and Ibn al-Uthaymeen as one of the three most influential Sunni scholars of the late 20th century.

title: Sheikh
bio_short_en: Syrian Hanafi hadith scholar who produced critical editions of major hadith works.
bio_en:
Abdul-Fattah Abu Ghuddah (1917-1997 CE / 1336-1417 AH) was a Syrian Hanafi scholar, hadith expert, and educator born in Aleppo. He studied under the leading scholars of Syria, including Ahmad al-Kurdi, Isa al-Bayanouni, and the great hadith master Muhammad Raghib at-Tabbakh. He also studied under Muhammad Zahid al-Kawthari in Egypt, who had a profound influence on his scholarly development.

Abu Ghuddah made his most lasting contributions through his meticulous critical editions of classical hadith and scholarly works. He edited and annotated al-Ajwibah al-Fadilah of al-Laknawi, Sharh Nukhbat al-Fikar of Ibn Hajar, al-Raf wat-Takmil of al-Laknawi, Qawa'id fi Ulum al-Hadith of al-Uthmani, and dozens of other classical texts. Each edition features detailed introductions, scholarly annotations, and biographical research on the authors. He also authored original works including Safahat min Sabr al-Ulama (Pages from the Patience of Scholars), a beloved collection of inspiring stories about scholarly perseverance.

Abu Ghuddah served in the Syrian parliament and was active in Islamic education and dawah before eventually settling in Saudi Arabia. He taught at the Imam Muhammad ibn Saud Islamic University in Riyadh and King Saud University. His editorial work set a standard for precision and thoroughness in Islamic publishing, and he trained a generation of students in the art of critical manuscript editing. He passed away in Riyadh and his critical editions remain among the most reliable available for the works he edited.

bio_short_en: Egyptian-Qatari scholar and chairman of the International Union of Muslim Scholars. Author of over 120 books including The Lawful and the Prohibited in Islam, and a prominent voice for centrist Islamic jurisprudence.
bio_en:
Yusuf ibn Abdullah al-Qaradawi (1926-2022 CE / 1345-1444 AH) was an Egyptian-born scholar who became one of the most widely known and prolific Islamic scholars of the contemporary era. Born in a village in the Nile Delta, he studied at al-Azhar and earned his doctorate from there. He later settled in Qatar, where he spent most of his professional life.

Al-Qaradawi authored over 120 books covering fiqh, theology, Quranic studies, economics, politics, and social issues. His most important work is al-Halal wal-Haram fil-Islam (The Lawful and the Prohibited in Islam), which became one of the most widely read Islamic books of the 20th century. His Fiqh az-Zakah is a comprehensive study of Islamic alms-giving, and Fiqh al-Awlawiyyat (Jurisprudence of Priorities) proposes a methodology for prioritizing Islamic rulings. His tafsir, completed later in life, was also widely circulated. He advocated for what he called wasatiyyah (moderation and balance) in Islamic thought.

Al-Qaradawi served as chairman of the International Union of Muslim Scholars and was the founding chairman of the European Council for Fatwa and Research. He reached millions through his television program ash-Shari'ah wal-Hayah (Islamic Law and Life) on Al Jazeera. His approach to fiqh emphasized ease over hardship, engagement with modern realities, and the objectives of Islamic law (maqasid ash-shari'ah). He was both widely followed and controversially received, with traditional scholars sometimes questioning his more lenient positions. He passed away in Doha, Qatar.

bio_short_en: Saudi scholar and former Mufti of the southern region of Saudi Arabia. Known for his teaching, fatwa work, and his commentary on Sunni creed texts.
bio_en:
Ahmad ibn Yahya ibn Muhammad an-Najmi (1927-2008 CE / 1346-1429 AH) was a Saudi scholar who served as the Mufti of the Jazan region in southern Saudi Arabia. Born in the village of Najamiyyah near Jazan, he studied under local scholars and later under the senior scholars of the kingdom, including Abdul-Aziz ibn Baz. He spent his career in teaching, issuing fatawa, and scholarly writing.

An-Najmi authored several works on Islamic creed and methodology, including al-Mawrid al-Adhb az-Zulal fi ma Intaqada ala Ba'd al-Manahij ad-Da'wiyyah min al-Aqwal wal-A'mal, a critique of what he viewed as methodological deviations in some Islamic movements. He also wrote Sharh Usul as-Sunnah lil-Imam Ahmad, a commentary on Imam Ahmad's creedal treatise, and al-Fatawa al-Jaliyyah, a collection of his legal opinions on various contemporary issues.

An-Najmi was known for his strict adherence to the Salafi methodology and was respected in the Jazan region as a senior scholarly authority. He was a member of the Dawah and Guidance Center in Jazan and trained numerous students. His works emphasize clarity in creedal matters and adherence to the understanding of the early generations of Muslims. He passed away in Jazan.

title: Sheikh
bio_short_en: Albanian-Syrian hadith scholar who produced definitive critical editions of Musnad Ahmad, Sahih Ibn Hibban, and many other works.
bio_en:
Shu'ayb ibn Muharram al-Arna'ut (1928-2016 CE / 1346-1438 AH) was an Albanian-origin hadith scholar who settled in Damascus, Syria, and became one of the most important hadith authenticators and manuscript editors of the modern era. Born in Albania, his family moved to Damascus when he was a child. He studied under Syrian scholars and devoted his life to hadith authentication and critical editing.

Al-Arnaut's most monumental achievement is his critical edition of Musnad Ahmad ibn Hanbal, a project that took over two decades and resulted in a fifty-volume edition with detailed authentication of every hadith. He also produced definitive editions of Sahih Ibn Hibban, Siyar A'lam an-Nubala of adh-Dhahabi, Sharh Mushkil al-Athar of at-Tahawi, Zad al-Ma'ad of Ibn al-Qayyim, Sharh as-Sunnah of al-Baghawi, and dozens of other classical works. His team-based approach to hadith verification set a new standard in the field.

Al-Arnaut's hadith gradings are widely respected and frequently cited alongside those of al-Albani, and where the two scholars differ, their respective analyses are carefully weighed by later researchers. His emphasis on rigorous methodology, combined with a balanced approach that respected the positions of all the major hadith scholars, earned him widespread trust. He was awarded the King Faisal International Prize for Islamic Studies in 2014 in recognition of his lifetime of service to hadith scholarship. He passed away in Amman, Jordan.

title: Sheikh
bio_short_en: A major Hanbali scholar known for his extensive commentary on jurisprudence and theology.
bio_en:
Muhammad ibn Salih ibn Muhammad ibn al-Uthaymeen (1929-2001 CE / 1347-1421 AH) was one of the most influential Sunni scholars of the 20th century, born in Unayzah in the Qasim region of Saudi Arabia. He studied under his townsman Abdurrahman as-Sa'di, who was his primary teacher, and later under Abdul-Aziz ibn Baz and Muhammad al-Amin ash-Shinqiti at the Islamic University of Madinah.

Ibn al-Uthaymeen was an extraordinarily prolific scholar whose works span virtually every field of Islamic knowledge. His most important works include ash-Sharh al-Mumti ala Zad al-Mustaqni, a comprehensive commentary on Hanbali fiqh that became a standard reference; Sharh al-Aqidah al-Wasitiyyah and Sharh Lum'at al-I'tiqad on Islamic creed; al-Usul min Ilm al-Usul on principles of jurisprudence; and Sharh Riyadh as-Salihin on the popular hadith collection. His Majmu Fatawa wa-Rasa'il covers over twenty volumes of legal opinions. His tafsir lectures, recorded over many years, were also compiled and published.

Ibn al-Uthaymeen served as imam of the Grand Mosque of Unayzah, professor at the Imam Muhammad ibn Saud Islamic University (Qasim branch), and member of the Council of Senior Scholars. He was known for his clarity of explanation, his deep compassion for questioners, and his ability to make complex juristic discussions accessible. Together with Ibn Baz and al-Albani, he is regarded as one of the three pillars of late 20th-century Sunni scholarship. His recorded lectures number in the thousands and continue to be widely distributed. He passed away in Jeddah.

bio_short_en: Ethiopian-born Shafi'i scholar who settled in Mecca and became a leading authority in Shafi'i fiqh and Ash'ari theology. Known for his encyclopedic work refuting deviant sects and his circles of learning at the Haram.
bio_en:
Muhammad ibn Abdullah al-Amin al-Harari al-Habashi (1929-2020 CE / 1348-1442 AH) was an Ethiopian-born Shafi'i scholar who settled in Mecca and became a respected authority in Shafi'i jurisprudence and Ash'ari theology. Born in the city of Harar in eastern Ethiopia, he received a thorough traditional education in the Islamic sciences before traveling to the Hijaz.

Al-Herari was a prolific author whose works cover fiqh, theology, and hadith. His Sharh Sahih Muslim is a detailed multi-volume commentary on the authentic hadith collection. He also authored al-Kawkab al-Wahhaj wal-Rawd al-Bahhaj fi Sharh Sahih Muslim, and works defending Ash'ari theological positions. He was known for his deep grounding in the traditional Shafi'i-Ash'ari scholarly approach and his mastery of Arabic despite being non-Arab by origin.

Al-Herari represented a current of traditional Sunni scholarship that emphasized the continuity of the Ash'ari-Maturidi theological heritage alongside adherence to the four established schools of jurisprudence. His works served as important references for scholars and students following the Shafi'i school, particularly in East Africa and the Hijaz. He spent decades teaching in Mecca and his students carried his scholarly tradition to various parts of the Muslim world. He passed away in Mecca.

bio_short_en: Syrian scholar and author of al-Fiqh al-Islami wa-Adillatuhu, a comprehensive comparative fiqh encyclopedia.
bio_en:
Wahba Mustafa az-Zuhayli (1932-2015 CE / 1351-1436 AH) was a Syrian scholar and one of the most prolific and respected Islamic jurists of the modern era. Born in Dair Atiyah near Damascus, he received both traditional Islamic education and modern academic training, earning a doctorate in Islamic law from al-Azhar University. He served as a professor at Damascus University's Faculty of Shariah for over four decades.

Az-Zuhayli's magnum opus is al-Fiqh al-Islami wa-Adillatuhu (Islamic Jurisprudence and its Evidences), an eight-volume encyclopedia that systematically presents the positions of all four Sunni schools of jurisprudence on every major legal question, along with their evidence. This work became the standard reference for comparative Islamic law in universities across the Muslim world. He also authored at-Tafsir al-Munir (The Illuminating Commentary), a comprehensive modern tafsir; al-Fiqh al-Islami fi Uslubih al-Jadid, a systematic presentation of Islamic law; and Athar al-Harb fil-Fiqh al-Islami on Islamic international law.

Az-Zuhayli authored over 200 books and research papers, making him one of the most productive Islamic scholars in history. He served on multiple international fiqh academies and Islamic finance boards. His ability to present classical Islamic law in a systematic, comparative, and accessible academic format made his works indispensable for modern students and practitioners of Islamic law. He was widely respected across different scholarly orientations for his balanced approach and encyclopedic knowledge. He passed away in Damascus.

bio_short_en: Saudi scholar and former head of the Sunnah department at the Islamic University of Medina. Known for his works on hadith methodology and his critiques of deviant groups.
bio_en:
Rabi' ibn Hadi Umayr al-Madkhali (b. 1932 CE / 1351 AH) is a Saudi hadith scholar who served as the head of the Sunnah department at the Islamic University of Madinah. Born in the Jazan region of southwestern Saudi Arabia, he studied under leading Saudi scholars including Abdul-Aziz ibn Baz, Muhammad Nasir ad-Din al-Albani, and Muhammad al-Amin ash-Shinqiti at the Islamic University of Madinah, where he later became a professor.

Al-Madkhali's scholarly works focus primarily on hadith methodology and the defense of the Sunnah. His most well-known works include Manhaj al-Anbiya fid-Da'wah ilallah fih al-Hikmah wal-Aql (The Methodology of the Prophets in Calling to Allah) and Bayn al-Imamain Muslim wad-Daraqutni, a study comparing the hadith methodologies of Imam Muslim and ad-Daraqutni. He has also written several works critiquing individuals and movements that he considers deviant from the Salafi methodology.

Al-Madkhali is a prominent figure within the Salafi movement and his scholarly opinions on matters of methodology and religious orientation carry significant weight among his followers. He has been a prolific teacher whose students extend across the Muslim world. His emphasis on strict adherence to the methodology of the early generations and his critiques of what he views as partisan movements have made him both influential and controversial within contemporary Islamic discourse.

bio_short_en: Syrian scholar and author of al-Fiqh al-Islami wa Adillatuh, a comprehensive encyclopedia of Islamic jurisprudence comparing all four madhabs. Also authored a major tafsir, at-Tafsir al-Munir.
bio_en:
Wahbah Mustafa az-Zuhayli (1932-2015 CE / 1351-1436 AH) was a leading Syrian Islamic jurist and professor of Islamic law at Damascus University who authored one of the most comprehensive works of comparative Islamic jurisprudence ever written. Born in Dair Atiyah near Damascus, he pursued Islamic studies and obtained his doctorate from al-Azhar. He combined deep traditional knowledge with modern academic methodology.

His al-Fiqh al-Islami wa Adillatuh (Islamic Jurisprudence and its Proofs) is an encyclopedic work covering the positions and evidence of all four Sunni schools of law on every major topic of jurisprudence. Running to eight volumes, it became the standard comparative fiqh reference in Islamic law faculties worldwide. His at-Tafsir al-Munir is a modern tafsir in sixteen volumes that integrates linguistic analysis, legal derivations, and practical lessons from the Quranic text. He also authored Usul al-Fiqh al-Islami on juristic methodology and numerous specialized studies on Islamic finance, international relations, and family law.

Az-Zuhayli was a member of numerous international Islamic fiqh academies, including the International Islamic Fiqh Academy in Jeddah. His ability to present the richness of the Islamic legal tradition in a structured, comparative format that is accessible to modern readers, jurists, and policymakers is his greatest legacy. He supervised hundreds of doctoral dissertations and his students occupy key academic and judicial positions across the Muslim world. He passed away in Damascus.

title: Sheikh
bio_short_en: Senior Saudi scholar and member of the Council of Senior Scholars, author of al-Mulakhkhas al-Fiqhi.
bio_en:
Salih ibn Fawzan ibn Abdullah al-Fawzan (b. 1933 CE / 1352 AH) is a senior Saudi scholar and member of the Council of Senior Scholars, the Permanent Committee for Islamic Research and Ifta, and several other prominent religious bodies in Saudi Arabia. Born in the village of ash-Shamasiyyah in the Qasim region, he studied at the Imam Muhammad ibn Saud Islamic University and later earned a doctorate in fiqh.

Al-Fawzan has authored numerous works on Islamic jurisprudence, creed, and contemporary issues. His most widely studied work is al-Mulakhkhas al-Fiqhi (The Concise Juristic Summary), a clear and comprehensive manual of Hanbali fiqh that became a standard textbook in Saudi Arabian religious institutions. He also authored Sharh al-Aqidah al-Wasitiyyah, a commentary on Ibn Taymiyyah's creedal text; I'anat al-Mustafid bi-Sharh Kitab at-Tawhid, a commentary on the creedal work of Muhammad ibn Abdul-Wahhab; and al-Irshad ila Sahih al-I'tiqad, a work on correct Islamic beliefs.

Al-Fawzan is one of the most prominent religious figures in Saudi Arabia and serves as a member of the highest religious advisory bodies in the kingdom. He has taught at the Imam Muhammad ibn Saud Islamic University for decades and his students include many of the current generation of Saudi scholars and judges. His works are widely used in Islamic educational curricula and his legal opinions carry significant weight in the Saudi scholarly establishment.

title: Sheikh
bio_short_en: Yemeni hadith scholar who established Dar al-Hadith in Dammaj, known for strict hadith methodology.
bio_en:
Muqbil ibn Hadi ibn Muqbil al-Wadi'i (1933-2001 CE / 1352-1422 AH) was a Yemeni hadith scholar who founded Dar al-Hadith in the village of Dammaj in Sa'dah province, Yemen. Born in the Wadi'ah area of Sa'dah, he traveled to Saudi Arabia where he studied at the Islamic University of Madinah under scholars including Abdul-Aziz ibn Baz and Muhammad Nasir ad-Din al-Albani.

Al-Wadi'i returned to Yemen and established Dar al-Hadith in Dammaj, which grew into one of the most important centers for hadith study in the Muslim world, attracting students from dozens of countries. His works include as-Sahih al-Musnad mimma laysa fi as-Sahihayn, a collection of authentic hadith not found in Bukhari and Muslim; ar-Riyad al-Jannah fir-Radd ala A'da as-Sunnah, a defense of the Sunnah; and ash-Shafa'ah, on the concept of intercession. He also authored works on hadith methodology and narrator criticism.

Al-Wadi'i was known for his strict adherence to hadith-based scholarship and his emphasis on authentic narrations in all matters of religion. Dar al-Hadith in Dammaj became a center of Salafi learning that produced graduates who went on to establish schools and circles of learning across Yemen, East Africa, and Southeast Asia. Despite operating in difficult conditions in one of Yemen's most remote regions, he built an institution of international significance. He passed away in Jeddah while receiving medical treatment.

title: Sheikh
bio_short_en: Saudi scholar known for his extensive fatwa collection and teaching.
bio_en:
Abdullah ibn Abdurrahman ibn Abdullah ibn Jibrin (1933-2009 CE / 1352-1430 AH) was a prominent Saudi scholar, teacher, and mufti known for his extensive fatawa and his tireless dedication to teaching. Born in the town of al-Quway'iyyah west of Riyadh, he studied under leading Saudi scholars including Muhammad ibn Ibrahim Al ash-Sheikh and Abdul-Aziz ibn Baz.

Ibn Jibrin served in various official religious capacities including as a member of the Permanent Committee for Islamic Research and Ifta and as a general supervisor of religious guidance. He was known for his vast knowledge of Hanbali fiqh and his willingness to engage with contemporary questions. His fatawa, collected in numerous volumes, address a wide range of practical issues faced by Muslims. He also authored commentaries on classical texts in creed and fiqh, including Sharh al-Aqidah at-Tahawiyyah and Sharh Akhsar al-Mukhtasarat in Hanbali fiqh.

Ibn Jibrin was beloved for his accessibility and his dedication to teaching, often traveling across Saudi Arabia to deliver lectures and answer questions. He was a regular participant in Islamic summer courses and educational programs. His gentle manner and extensive knowledge made him one of the most popular scholars among the Saudi public. He passed away in Riyadh.

bio_short_en: Yemeni hadith scholar who established Dar al-Hadith in Dammaj, Yemen. Known for his work in hadith authentication and his efforts to spread the Sunnah in Yemen.
bio_en:
Muqbil ibn Hadi al-Wadi'i (1933-2001 CE / 1352-1422 AH) was a prominent Yemeni scholar and hadith specialist who established one of the most significant institutions for hadith study in the contemporary Muslim world. Born in the Wadi'ah region of Sa'dah province in northern Yemen, he traveled to Saudi Arabia for education, studying at the Islamic University of Madinah under scholars such as Ibn Baz and al-Albani, before returning to Yemen to establish his school.

His center, Dar al-Hadith in Dammaj, attracted thousands of students from across the Muslim world who came to study hadith sciences, Arabic, and other Islamic disciplines in an intensive traditional setting. His published works include as-Sahih al-Musnad min Asbab an-Nuzul on the authentic occasions of Quranic revelation, al-Jami as-Sahih mimma laysa fis-Sahihayn on authentic hadith outside Bukhari and Muslim, and al-Mustalah on hadith terminology. He also authored works critiquing movements and ideas he considered deviations from Salafi methodology.

Al-Wadi'i's impact on Islamic education in Yemen was transformative. Before his school, Yemen had limited access to hadith-based scholarship. The graduates of Dammaj established study centers and mosques across Yemen and in other countries, creating a network of hadith education that continues today. He was known for his asceticism, his dedication to knowledge, and his refusal of worldly comforts despite the support he received. He passed away in Jeddah while being treated for illness.

bio_short_en: Saudi hadith scholar and former vice-chancellor of the Islamic University of Medina. Known for his extensive commentary on Sunan Abu Dawud and other hadith works.
bio_en:
Abdul-Muhsin ibn Hamad ibn Abdul-Muhsin al-Abbad al-Badr (b. 1934 CE / 1353 AH) is a Saudi hadith scholar who served as the vice-chancellor of the Islamic University of Madinah and has been one of the most prominent teachers at the Prophet's Mosque. Born in Zulfi in the Riyadh region, he studied under leading Saudi scholars and has devoted his life to teaching hadith and its related sciences.

Al-Abbad's most important scholarly contribution is his extensive commentary on Sunan Abi Dawud, delivered as lectures over many years at the Prophet's Mosque in Madinah and compiled as Sharh Sunan Abi Dawud. This multi-volume work provides detailed explanation of the hadith texts, their chains of narration, and their legal implications. He has also authored Sharh Arba'in an-Nawawiyyah, a commentary on an-Nawawi's forty hadith, and numerous treatises on creedal topics and scholarly methodology.

Al-Abbad played a central role in shaping the Islamic University of Madinah during its formative years and has been one of the most senior and respected teachers in the Prophet's Mosque for decades. His lectures are attended by students and scholars from around the world, and his recordings are widely distributed. He is known for his balanced approach, his gentleness with students, and his vast knowledge of hadith and the biographical literature of narrators. He continues to teach and contribute to Islamic scholarship from Madinah.

title: Mufti
bio_short_en: Pakistani Hanafi scholar, former judge of the Shariah Appellate Bench, and a leading authority on Islamic finance.
bio_en:
Muhammad Taqi ibn Muhammad Shafi Usmani (b. 1943 CE / 1362 AH) is a Pakistani Hanafi scholar, former judge of the Shariah Appellate Bench of the Supreme Court of Pakistan, and one of the world's foremost authorities on Islamic finance. Born in Deoband, India (before the partition), he is the son of the renowned Mufti Muhammad Shafi, the first Grand Mufti of Pakistan and author of Ma'arif al-Quran.

Mufti Taqi Usmani studied at Darul Uloom Karachi and also earned a law degree, combining traditional Islamic scholarship with modern legal training. His scholarly works include Takmilat Fath al-Mulhim, completing the commentary on Sahih Muslim that was begun by al-Kashmiri and continued by his father; Buhuth fi Qadaya Fiqhiyyah Mu'asirah (Discussions on Contemporary Juristic Issues), addressing modern legal questions from an Islamic perspective; and In'am al-Bari, lectures on Sahih al-Bukhari. His works in Urdu, including Taqi's commentary on the Quran and his travelogues, are also widely read.

Mufti Taqi Usmani is considered the architect of modern Islamic finance. He served as chairman or member of Shariah advisory boards for numerous Islamic banks, financial institutions, and the Accounting and Auditing Organization for Islamic Financial Institutions (AAOIFI). His legal opinions and scholarly framework for Islamic banking, sukuk (Islamic bonds), and other financial instruments have shaped the multi-trillion-dollar Islamic finance industry. He continues to serve as a global authority on Hanafi jurisprudence and Islamic finance from Karachi.

title: Sheikh
bio_short_en: Saudi scholar and member of the Council of Senior Scholars, author of works on etiquette and fiqh terminology.
bio_en:
Bakr ibn Abdullah Abu Zayd ibn Muhammad ibn Abdullah ibn Bakr (1944-2008 CE / 1364-1429 AH) was a Saudi scholar and member of the Council of Senior Scholars, the Permanent Committee for Islamic Research and Ifta, and the International Islamic Fiqh Academy. Born in Shaqra in the Riyadh region, he studied in Riyadh and at the Islamic University of Madinah and later served as a judge before turning fully to scholarly work.

Bakr Abu Zayd authored distinctive works that addressed topics often neglected by other scholars. His most beloved work is Hilyat Talib al-Ilm (The Adornment of the Student of Knowledge), a guide to the etiquette and manners of seeking Islamic knowledge that has been widely adopted as essential reading for beginning students. He also authored al-Madkhal al-Mufassal ila Fiqh al-Imam Ahmad ibn Hanbal, a detailed introduction to Hanbali jurisprudence and its bibliography; Mu'jam al-Manahi al-Lafziyyah (Dictionary of Prohibited Expressions), identifying common phrases that carry problematic meanings; Tashih ad-Du'a, correcting common errors in supplication; and at-Ta'sil li-Usul at-Takhrij on hadith referencing methodology.

Bakr Abu Zayd's works are characterized by their originality, their attention to scholarly manners and methodology, and their practical utility. His Hilyat Talib al-Ilm in particular has become a foundational text in Islamic educational curricula worldwide. He served in the highest religious advisory positions in Saudi Arabia and was respected across scholarly orientations for his fairness, erudition, and commitment to scholarly standards. He passed away in Riyadh.

bio_short_en: American convert to Islam and Shafi'i scholar based in Amman. Translated the Reliance of the Traveller (Umdat as-Salik), a major Shafi'i fiqh manual, and is a murshid in the Shadhili Sufi order.
bio_en:
Nuh Ha Mim Keller (b. 1954 CE / 1374 AH) is an American scholar who converted to Islam in 1977 and became a leading Shafi'i jurist, Arabic translator, and Sufi shaykh. Born in the rural northwestern United States, he studied philosophy and Arabic at the University of Chicago and later traveled to Syria and Jordan to study Islamic sciences under traditional scholars, including Abdul-Rahman ash-Shaghouri, a renowned Syrian Sufi master.

Keller's most significant contribution to English-language Islamic scholarship is his translation of Umdat as-Salik wa-Uddat an-Nasik (Reliance of the Traveller), the classic Shafi'i legal manual by Ahmad ibn Naqib al-Misri. This translation, certified by al-Azhar University, became the first Islamic legal work in a European language to receive such certification and remains the standard English reference for Shafi'i fiqh. He also translated and edited Imam an-Nawawi's al-Adhkar (The Book of Remembrances) and wrote several influential essays on Islamic creed, Sufism, and the scholarly tradition.

Keller is based in Amman, Jordan, where he teaches Islamic sciences to an international community of students. He is an initiated shaykh of the Shadhili Sufi order and emphasizes the complementarity of Shariah, Sufism, and traditional Ash'ari-Maturidi theology. His works and teachings have been particularly influential among English-speaking Muslims seeking to engage with traditional Islamic scholarship. He has played a significant role in making classical Islamic legal and spiritual texts accessible to Western audiences.

name_full_en: Muhammad al-Hasan ibn al-Khadim Walad ad-Dadaw ash-Shinqiti
title: Sheikh
bio_short_en: Mauritanian polymath scholar known for his encyclopedic knowledge across all Islamic sciences.
bio_en:
Muhammad al-Hasan Walad ad-Dadaw ash-Shinqiti (b. 1963 CE / 1383 AH) is a Mauritanian scholar widely recognized as one of the most learned Muslim scholars alive today. Born into a prominent scholarly family in Mauritania, he was raised in the rigorous Mauritanian tradition of memorization and oral scholarship. He is said to have memorized the Quran, thousands of hadith, extensive classical poetry, and numerous major texts in fiqh, usul, grammar, and other sciences.

Walad ad-Dadaw's encyclopedic knowledge encompasses virtually every traditional Islamic discipline: Quranic sciences, hadith, fiqh (with mastery of all four schools, though he is primarily Maliki), usul al-fiqh, Arabic grammar and rhetoric, seerah, and Islamic history. He teaches and lectures extensively, and his recorded lessons and lectures number in the thousands. While he has not produced traditional written scholarly works on the scale of earlier scholars, his oral contributions and fatawa have been widely compiled and distributed.

He is the founder and president of Markaz Takwin al-Ulama (Center for Scholar Formation) in Mauritania, dedicated to training the next generation of scholars. His extraordinary memory, ability to cite sources across all the Islamic sciences from memory, and mastery of the Arabic language have earned him widespread admiration across the Muslim world. Scholars from various orientations recognize his learning, and he regularly participates in international Islamic conferences and scholarly gatherings. He represents the continuation of the Mauritanian scholarly tradition that produced figures like Muhammad al-Amin ash-Shinqiti.

title: Mufti
bio_short_en: British Hanafi scholar and founder of Darul Iftaa, known for contemporary fiqh rulings.
bio_en:
Muhammad ibn Adam al-Kawthari (b. 1975 CE / 1395 AH) is a British Hanafi scholar of Indo-Pakistani origin based in Leicester, England. He studied traditional Islamic sciences at Darul Uloom in Bury, England, and later at various institutions in the Muslim world, including study with scholars in Syria, Pakistan, and elsewhere. He is named al-Kawthari in tribute to the Ottoman scholar Muhammad Zahid al-Kawthari.

Muhammad ibn Adam founded Darul Iftaa (Institute of Islamic Jurisprudence) in Leicester, which provides Islamic legal opinions and educational resources. He has authored and contributed to several works on contemporary Hanafi fiqh, including Islamic Guide to Sexual Relations, which addresses intimate matters from an Islamic perspective in a direct and scholarly manner, and Birth Control & Abortion in Islam. He also maintains an extensive online fatwa archive that addresses thousands of contemporary questions in Islamic jurisprudence.

His scholarly approach combines classical Hanafi legal methodology with sensitivity to the challenges faced by Muslims living in the West. He is a popular lecturer and teacher who travels internationally to deliver courses on fiqh, hadith, and Islamic spirituality. His ability to address contemporary issues such as medical ethics, financial transactions, and family law while remaining grounded in the classical Hanafi tradition has made him one of the most sought-after scholars in the English-speaking Muslim world.

name_ar: عائشة بنت محمد بن عبد الهادي
name_en: Aisha bint Muhammad ibn Abd al-Hadi
name_full_en: Aisha bint Muhammad ibn Abd al-Hadi al-Maqdisiyya
bio_short_en: One of the most prominent female hadith scholars of the 8th-9th century AH, who held ijazahs from major scholars and taught students including Ibn Hajar al-Asqalani.
bio_en:
Aisha bint Muhammad ibn Abd al-Hadi al-Maqdisiyya (1323-1413 CE / 723-816 AH) was one of the most distinguished female hadith scholars of her era. Born into the prominent Maqdisi scholarly family of Damascus, she received ijazahs (scholarly licenses) from many of the greatest hadith masters of her time, including al-Mizzi and adh-Dhahabi.

She became renowned for her mastery of hadith sciences and her high chains of narration (isnad ali), which were highly sought after by students of knowledge. Among her most famous students was the great hadith master Ibn Hajar al-Asqalani, who studied under her and transmitted hadith through her chains. She taught at the Umayyad Mosque in Damascus and other learning centers.

Her career exemplifies the long tradition of female scholarship in Islam. She lived to the age of approximately 90, and her longevity meant that she held some of the highest chains of transmission available in her time, making her an essential link in the preservation of hadith literature.

name_full_en: Fatimah bint Sa'd al-Khayr ibn Muhammad al-Ansariyyah
bio_short_en: A distinguished female hadith scholar from the Hanbali tradition who narrated from major authorities of her time.
bio_en:
Fatimah bint Sa'd al-Khayr ibn Muhammad al-Ansariyyah (c. 1100-1178 CE / 494-574 AH) was a celebrated female hadith scholar who traveled in pursuit of knowledge and acquired ijazahs from prominent scholars across the Muslim world. She studied under numerous hadith masters and became known for the high quality of her chains of narration.

Scholars of her time recognized her reliability and erudition, and she taught hadith to many students. Her role in the scholarly tradition reflects the significant contributions of women to Islamic learning during the classical and medieval periods, particularly in the hadith sciences where female scholars were respected and sought out for their knowledge.

name_full_en: Abu al-Qasim Abd al-Karim ibn Hawazin al-Qushayri
title: Imam
bio_short_en: Author of 'ar-Risalah al-Qushayriyyah,' one of the most important classical texts on Sufism and spiritual ethics.
bio_en:
Abu al-Qasim Abd al-Karim ibn Hawazin al-Qushayri (986-1072 CE / 376-465 AH) was a prominent Shafi'i jurist, Ash'ari theologian, and Sufi master best known for his seminal work 'ar-Risalah al-Qushayriyyah' (The Epistle on Sufism). Born in Khurasan, he studied under the great Sufi master Abu Ali ad-Daqqaq, whose daughter he married.

His 'Risalah' is considered one of the most authoritative classical texts on Sufism. It systematically presents the stations and states of the spiritual path, biographies of early Sufi masters, and explanations of key Sufi terminology. The work is notable for grounding Sufi concepts firmly within orthodox Sunni theology and jurisprudence.

Al-Qushayri was also an accomplished hadith scholar and Quran commentator, authoring a tafsir titled 'Lata'if al-Isharat' (Subtle Allusions), which interprets the Quran from a spiritual perspective. He traveled extensively, teaching in Baghdad, Mecca, and other centers of learning. His integration of legal, theological, and spiritual sciences made him one of the most well-rounded scholars of his era.

name_full_en: Shihab ad-Din Abu Hafs Umar as-Suhrawardi
title: Sheikh
bio_short_en: Founder of the Suhrawardiyyah Sufi order and author of 'Awarif al-Ma'arif,' a key text on Sufi practice and ethics.
bio_en:
Shihab ad-Din Abu Hafs Umar as-Suhrawardi (1145-1234 CE / 539-632 AH) was a major Sufi master, Shafi'i jurist, and the systematizer of the Suhrawardiyyah Sufi order. Born in Suhraward (modern Iran), he was raised by his uncle Abu an-Najib as-Suhrawardi, also a renowned Sufi scholar.

His most famous work, 'Awarif al-Ma'arif' (The Benefits of Spiritual Knowledge), became one of the foundational manuals of Sufi practice. The text covers the etiquette of discipleship, spiritual exercises, the rules of Sufi gatherings, and the relationship between Islamic law and spiritual practice. It was widely studied across the Muslim world and remains influential.

As-Suhrawardi held significant influence in Baghdad, serving as an envoy of the Abbasid Caliph to various Muslim rulers. He was respected by scholars of all disciplines for his learning and piety. The Suhrawardiyyah order he organized spread throughout the Muslim world, particularly in the Indian subcontinent, where it remains one of the major Sufi orders.

name_full_en: Abu al-Mawahib Abd al-Wahhab ibn Ahmad ash-Sha'rani
title: Imam
bio_short_en: Egyptian Sufi scholar and prolific author known for 'al-Tabaqat al-Kubra' and 'al-Mizan al-Kubra' on comparative fiqh.
bio_en:
Abu al-Mawahib Abd al-Wahhab ibn Ahmad ash-Sha'rani (1493-1565 CE / 898-973 AH) was a prominent Egyptian Sufi scholar, jurist, and prolific author. He was a Shafi'i jurist who also studied the other three madhabs extensively, making him one of the foremost authorities on comparative Islamic jurisprudence of his era.

His 'al-Mizan al-Kubra' (The Great Balance) is a landmark work in comparative fiqh, presenting the positions of all four Sunni schools of law and arguing for the validity of each within their respective frameworks. His 'al-Tabaqat al-Kubra' (The Great Biographical Dictionary) is an extensive biographical dictionary of Sufi saints and scholars, providing invaluable historical information about the spiritual masters of Egypt and the broader Muslim world.

Ash-Sha'rani was a leading figure in Egyptian religious life during the early Ottoman period. He authored over 300 works on topics ranging from jurisprudence and hadith to Sufi practice and ethics. His writings consistently sought to harmonize the legal and spiritual dimensions of Islam.

name_full_en: Abu Amr Uthman ibn Sa'id ad-Dani
title: Imam
bio_short_en: Leading authority on Quranic recitations (qira'at) and author of 'at-Taysir,' a foundational text in the science of Quranic readings.
bio_en:
Abu Amr Uthman ibn Sa'id ad-Dani (981-1053 CE / 371-444 AH) was one of the most eminent scholars of Quranic recitations (qira'at) in Islamic history. Born in Cordoba, al-Andalus, he studied under numerous masters of Quranic sciences and traveled extensively in pursuit of knowledge.

His work 'at-Taysir fi al-Qira'at as-Sab' (The Facilitation of the Seven Readings) became one of the most important reference works on the seven canonical Quranic recitations. The book served as the primary textbook for students of qira'at for centuries. He also authored works on Quranic orthography, verse counting, and the sciences of tajwid.

Ad-Dani settled in Denia (Daniyyah) on the eastern coast of al-Andalus, from which his nisba derives. He was a Maliki jurist and hadith scholar in addition to his expertise in Quranic sciences. His contributions to the preservation and systemization of Quranic readings remain foundational in the field to this day.

name_full_en: Shams ad-Din Abu al-Khayr Muhammad ibn Muhammad al-Jazari
title: Imam
bio_short_en: The greatest authority on Quranic recitations, author of 'an-Nashr fi al-Qira'at al-Ashr' and the 'Jazariyyah' poem on tajwid.
bio_en:
Shams ad-Din Abu al-Khayr Muhammad ibn Muhammad al-Jazari (1350-1429 CE / 751-833 AH) is universally regarded as the greatest authority on Quranic recitations (qira'at) in Islamic history. Born in Damascus, he memorized the Quran as a child and studied all known recitations from the leading masters of his time.

His magnum opus, 'an-Nashr fi al-Qira'at al-Ashr' (The Dissemination of the Ten Readings), is the most comprehensive work ever written on the subject of Quranic recitations. It documents the ten canonical readings and their sub-narrations with meticulous detail. He also composed 'Tayyibat an-Nashr,' a versified summary of the same work, and the famous 'al-Muqaddimah al-Jazariyyah,' a didactic poem on the rules of tajwid that is still memorized and taught in Quran schools worldwide.

Ibn al-Jazari traveled extensively, teaching in Damascus, Cairo, Anatolia, and Shiraz. He established schools for Quran recitation and trained generations of students. His biographical dictionary 'Ghayat an-Nihayah fi Tabaqat al-Qurra' catalogs thousands of Quran reciters throughout history. He is considered the seal of the huffaz (masters of Quranic memorization) and his works remain the ultimate reference in qira'at sciences.

name_full_en: Abu al-Hasan Ali ibn Ahmad al-Wahidi an-Naysaburi
title: Imam
bio_short_en: Pioneer in the science of 'Asbab an-Nuzul' (occasions of revelation) and author of three major Quran commentaries.
bio_en:
Abu al-Hasan Ali ibn Ahmad al-Wahidi an-Naysaburi (1008-1076 CE / 398-468 AH) was a leading Quran commentator and pioneer of the science of Asbab an-Nuzul (the occasions and contexts of Quranic revelation). Born in Nishapur, he studied under prominent scholars including the great linguist Tha'labi.

His most influential work is 'Asbab an-Nuzul,' which systematically records the historical circumstances surrounding the revelation of Quranic verses. This work became the standard reference in its field and has been used by every subsequent Quran commentator. He also authored three tafsirs of varying length: 'al-Basit' (the extensive), 'al-Wasit' (the intermediate), and 'al-Wajiz' (the concise).

Al-Wahidi was also accomplished in Arabic language and literature. His work on the occasions of revelation helped establish a critical methodology for understanding the Quran in its historical context, a science that remains essential in Islamic scholarship.

name_full_en: Muhammad Nawawi ibn Umar al-Jawi al-Bantani
title: Sheikh
bio_short_en: The most influential Southeast Asian Islamic scholar, based in Mecca, whose commentaries on classical texts are still widely used across the Malay world.
bio_en:
Muhammad Nawawi ibn Umar al-Jawi al-Bantani (1813-1897 CE / 1230-1314 AH) was the most prominent Southeast Asian Islamic scholar of the 19th century. Born in Tanara, Banten (western Java, modern Indonesia), he traveled to Mecca as a young man and spent most of his life teaching at the Masjid al-Haram.

He authored over 100 works in Arabic, primarily commentaries (sharh) on classical Islamic texts spanning tafsir, hadith, jurisprudence, theology, and Sufism. His Quran commentary 'Marah Labid' (also known as 'Tafsir al-Munir') is one of the most widely studied tafsirs in Southeast Asia. His commentaries on legal texts such as 'Tuhfat al-Murid' (on Jawharat at-Tawhid) and 'Nihayat az-Zayn' (on Qurrat al-Ayn) became standard textbooks in Islamic boarding schools (pesantren) across Indonesia, Malaysia, and Thailand.

Al-Bantani was a Shafi'i jurist and Ash'ari theologian who played a pivotal role in shaping Islamic education in the Malay archipelago. His students returned from Mecca to establish pesantren across Southeast Asia, creating a vast network of Islamic learning that endures to this day. He is considered the intellectual father of the Indonesian pesantren tradition.

name_full_en: Ahmad Khatib ibn Abd al-Latif al-Minangkabawi
title: Sheikh
bio_short_en: Minangkabau scholar who served as Imam of the Shafi'i school at Masjid al-Haram and mentored major Indonesian reformist scholars.
bio_en:
Ahmad Khatib ibn Abd al-Latif al-Minangkabawi (1860-1916 CE / 1276-1334 AH) was a prominent Indonesian scholar from the Minangkabau region of West Sumatra who became one of the leading Shafi'i scholars at the Masjid al-Haram in Mecca. He held the position of imam for the Shafi'i maqam at the Grand Mosque, a remarkable achievement for a scholar from Southeast Asia.

He is particularly significant for his role in training the next generation of Indonesian Islamic reformers and scholars. His students include Ahmad Dahlan (founder of Muhammadiyah), Hasyim Asy'ari (founder of Nahdlatul Ulama), and other figures who would shape Indonesian Islam in the 20th century. Through these students, his influence touched virtually every major Islamic movement in Indonesia.

Ahmad Khatib wrote on jurisprudence, theology, and social issues affecting the Malay Muslim community. He engaged in debates about the compatibility of Minangkabau customary law (adat) with Islamic inheritance law, advocating for strict adherence to Shafi'i legal positions.

bio_short_en: Indonesian Islamic scholar, author, and political figure, known for his major Quran commentary 'Tafsir al-Azhar.'
bio_en:
Haji Abdul Malik Karim Amrullah, known by the acronym Hamka (1908-1981 CE / 1326-1401 AH), was one of the most influential Indonesian Islamic scholars, authors, and public intellectuals of the 20th century. Born in Maninjau, West Sumatra, he was the son of the reformist scholar Abdul Karim Amrullah.

Hamka is best known for his monumental Quran commentary 'Tafsir al-Azhar,' a comprehensive work in the Indonesian language that makes Quranic scholarship accessible to Malay-speaking Muslims. He began writing it during his imprisonment under the Sukarno government (1964-1966) and completed it after his release. The tafsir combines traditional exegesis with contemporary relevance and remains one of the most widely read Quran commentaries in Southeast Asia.

He served as the first chairman of the Indonesian Ulema Council (MUI) and was a prominent leader of the Muhammadiyah movement. Beyond religious scholarship, Hamka was a celebrated novelist, essayist, and journalist. His novel 'Tenggelamnya Kapal van der Wijck' is considered a classic of Indonesian literature. He received honorary doctorates from Al-Azhar University and other institutions.

name_full_en: Ahmad ibn Muhammad ibn Habibullah Mbake (Ahmad Bamba)
title: Sheikh
bio_short_en: Senegalese Sufi scholar and founder of the Muridiyyah order, known for his pacifist resistance to French colonialism.
bio_en:
Ahmad ibn Muhammad ibn Habibullah, known as Ahmad Bamba or Serigne Touba (1853-1927 CE / 1270-1346 AH), was a Senegalese Islamic scholar, Sufi master, and the founder of the Muridiyyah Sufi order. Born in Mbacke, Senegal, into a scholarly family of the Wolof people, he devoted himself from a young age to Islamic learning and spiritual practice.

Ahmad Bamba is celebrated for his principled, non-violent resistance to French colonial rule in Senegal. Despite being exiled multiple times, first to Gabon (1895-1902) and then to Mauritania, he refused to take up arms, instead emphasizing spiritual struggle (jihad an-nafs) and the pursuit of knowledge. His steadfastness during exile became legendary and attracted a massive following.

He authored numerous works on theology, jurisprudence, and Sufism in Arabic. His teachings emphasized the centrality of work and service as forms of worship. He founded the holy city of Touba, which hosts the Grand Mosque and the annual Grand Magal pilgrimage, one of the largest annual gatherings in Africa. The Muridiyyah order remains one of the most influential Islamic movements in West Africa, with millions of followers.

name_full_en: Uthman ibn Muhammad ibn Uthman ibn Salih (dan Fodio)
title: Sheikh
bio_short_en: Fulani scholar, reformer, and founder of the Sokoto Caliphate, one of the largest states in 19th-century Africa.
bio_en:
Uthman ibn Muhammad, known as Uthman dan Fodio (1754-1817 CE / 1168-1232 AH), was a Fulani Islamic scholar, teacher, reformer, and revolutionary who founded the Sokoto Caliphate in what is now northern Nigeria. He was one of the most significant Islamic figures in West African history.

Born into a scholarly Fulani family in Gobir (modern northern Nigeria), he studied under numerous scholars and began a career of preaching and teaching that attracted a large following. His calls for Islamic reform, opposition to syncretistic practices, and advocacy for justice brought him into conflict with the Hausa ruling class. In 1804, he led a jihad that resulted in the establishment of the Sokoto Caliphate, which became one of the largest and most organized states in Africa.

Dan Fodio was a prolific author who wrote over 100 works in Arabic, Fulfulde, and Hausa on Islamic theology, jurisprudence, governance, education, and women's rights in Islam. He was a Maliki jurist and Qadiri Sufi who emphasized the importance of educating both men and women. His daughter Nana Asma'u became a renowned scholar in her own right. The Sokoto Caliphate endured until British colonization in 1903, and his legacy continues to shape Islamic practice in West Africa.

name_full_en: Abdullah ibn Muhammad ibn Uthman ibn Salih (ibn Fudi)
title: Sheikh
bio_short_en: Brother of Uthman dan Fodio, a major West African scholar, poet, and military commander of the Sokoto Caliphate.
bio_en:
Abdullah ibn Muhammad ibn Uthman, known as Abdullah ibn Fudi (1766-1829 CE / 1180-1245 AH), was a leading West African Islamic scholar, poet, military commander, and the brother of Uthman dan Fodio. He played a central role in the Sokoto Jihad and the administration of the resulting caliphate.

Abdullah was a formidable scholar who authored works on jurisprudence, grammar, history, and poetry. His historical chronicle 'Tazyin al-Waraqat' provides a first-hand account of the Sokoto Jihad and is one of the most important primary sources for the history of the movement. He was also a skilled military strategist who led forces in key campaigns.

As Emir of Gwandu, he administered the western provinces of the Sokoto Caliphate. He was a Maliki jurist and a master of Arabic language and poetry. His scholarly output, combined with his practical leadership, made him one of the most accomplished figures in the intellectual history of West Africa.

name_full_en: Nana Asma'u bint Uthman dan Fodio
bio_short_en: Daughter of Uthman dan Fodio and a celebrated female scholar, poet, and educator who established women's education networks in the Sokoto Caliphate.
bio_en:
Nana Asma'u bint Uthman dan Fodio (1793-1864 CE / 1208-1280 AH) was a renowned West African female scholar, poet, teacher, and the daughter of Uthman dan Fodio, the founder of the Sokoto Caliphate. She was one of the most accomplished women in 19th-century African and Islamic history.

Fluent in Arabic, Fulfulde, Hausa, and Tamachek, Nana Asma'u authored numerous works of poetry and prose on Islamic theology, jurisprudence, history, and education. She established the 'Yan Taru' (the Associates), a network of women teachers who traveled to rural communities to educate women in Islamic knowledge, a system that continues in modified form in northern Nigeria to this day.

Her poetry celebrated the achievements of the Sokoto movement, memorialized its leaders, and taught Islamic principles in accessible verse. She corresponded with scholars across the Muslim world and was recognized as a major intellectual figure during her lifetime. Her legacy demonstrates the long tradition of female Islamic scholarship in West Africa and serves as an inspiration for women's education initiatives globally.

name_full_en: Muhammad Sa'id ibn Mulla Ramadan al-Buti
title: Sheikh
bio_short_en: Leading Syrian Shafi'i scholar and professor at Damascus University, author of numerous influential books on Islamic thought and jurisprudence.
bio_en:
Muhammad Sa'id ibn Mulla Ramadan al-Buti (1929-2013 CE / 1348-1434 AH) was one of the most prominent Sunni scholars of the late 20th and early 21st centuries. Born in Jilka, a Kurdish village in Turkey, his family migrated to Damascus when he was four years old. He studied at al-Azhar University and earned a doctorate in Islamic jurisprudence.

Al-Buti served as the head of the Department of Islamic Theology (Aqeedah and Religions) at the University of Damascus and was a prolific author of over 60 books. His works cover theology, jurisprudence, Quranic sciences, and responses to contemporary intellectual challenges. Notable titles include 'al-Lamadhhabiyyah' (criticizing the rejection of following a madhab), 'Fiqh as-Sirah' (Jurisprudence of the Prophetic Biography), and 'Kubra al-Yaqiniyyat al-Kawniyyah' (The Great Universal Certainties).

He was a Shafi'i jurist, Ash'ari theologian, and a defender of traditional Sunni scholarship against both modernist and literalist trends. He delivered regular Friday sermons at the Umayyad Mosque in Damascus and had a massive following across the Arab world through his television appearances and lectures. He was killed in a bombing at the Iman Mosque in Damascus in 2013.

name_full_en: Ali ibn Nayif ibn Ahmad Gomaa
title: Sheikh
bio_short_en: Former Grand Mufti of Egypt, Shafi'i jurist and Azhari scholar known for his traditional Islamic scholarship and engagement with contemporary issues.
bio_en:
Ali ibn Nayif ibn Ahmad Gomaa (b. 1952 CE / 1371 AH) is an Egyptian Islamic scholar who served as the Grand Mufti of Egypt from 2003 to 2013. Born in Bani Suwaif, Upper Egypt, he studied at al-Azhar University, where he earned his doctorate in Islamic jurisprudence.

As Grand Mufti, he headed Dar al-Ifta al-Misriyyah (the Egyptian House of Fatwa), one of the most important institutions of Islamic legal authority in the Sunni world. He issued thousands of fatwas on contemporary issues and worked to make Islamic legal reasoning accessible to the modern Muslim public.

Gomaa is a Shafi'i jurist and Ash'ari theologian with deep expertise in usul al-fiqh (principles of Islamic jurisprudence). He is a prolific author and has written extensively on Islamic theology, spirituality, and the application of Islamic law in modern contexts. He is a professor at al-Azhar University and continues to teach and lecture widely.

title: Sheikh
bio_short_en: Grand Imam of al-Azhar since 2010, one of the most influential Sunni Muslim leaders in the world.
bio_en:
Ahmad Muhammad Ahmad al-Tayyib (b. 1946 CE / 1365 AH) is the current Grand Imam of al-Azhar and the President of al-Azhar University, making him one of the most prominent figures in Sunni Islam globally. Born in Luxor, Upper Egypt, into a family known for scholarship and Sufi learning, he studied at al-Azhar and earned his doctorate from the Sorbonne in Paris.

As Grand Imam of al-Azhar since 2010, he has been a leading voice for interfaith dialogue, moderation, and the renewal of Islamic scholarship. He played a key role in drafting the 'Document on Human Fraternity for World Peace and Living Together' alongside Pope Francis in Abu Dhabi in 2019.

Al-Tayyib is an Ash'ari theologian with specialization in Islamic philosophy and logic. He has served in various academic and administrative roles at al-Azhar, including as President of al-Azhar University and as Grand Mufti of Egypt (2002-2003). He advocates for the centrality of al-Azhar as a global institution of moderate Sunni scholarship.

title: Sheikh
bio_short_en: British Muslim scholar, Dean of the Cambridge Muslim College, and one of the most influential Western Muslim intellectuals.
bio_en:
Timothy John Winter, known as Abdal Hakim Murad (b. 1960 CE / 1380 AH), is a British Muslim scholar, academic, and one of the most influential Muslim thinkers in the Western world. Born in London, he studied Arabic at the University of Cambridge and later pursued traditional Islamic studies in Cairo and Jeddah under renowned scholars.

He is the Shaykh Zayed Lecturer in Islamic Studies at the University of Cambridge, a Fellow of Wolfson College, and the founder and Dean of the Cambridge Muslim College, which trains Muslim religious leaders for service in Britain. He has translated several classical Islamic texts into English, including works by al-Ghazali from the 'Ihya Ulum ad-Din.'

Abdal Hakim Murad has been consistently listed among the world's most influential Muslims. His lectures and writings address the challenges facing Muslims in the modern West, the relationship between Islam and modernity, and the importance of traditional Islamic scholarship. He is a Hanafi in fiqh, Maturidi in aqeedah, and has deep engagement with the Sufi tradition. His book 'Travelling Home: Essays on Islam in Europe' is widely regarded as essential reading on Islam in the Western context.

name_full_en: Umar ibn Muhammad ibn Salim ibn Hafiz ibn Abdullah
title: Habib
bio_short_en: Yemeni scholar and founder of Dar al-Mustafa in Tarim, one of the most influential traditional Islamic learning centers in the world.
bio_en:
Umar ibn Muhammad ibn Salim ibn Hafiz (b. 1963 CE / 1383 AH), commonly known as Habib Umar bin Hafiz, is one of the most influential living Islamic scholars. Born in Tarim, Hadhramaut, Yemen, into the Ba'Alawi sada family that traces its lineage to the Prophet Muhammad through Husayn ibn Ali, he grew up in a household of deep learning and spirituality. His father, Muhammad ibn Salim bin Hafiz, was a prominent scholar who was killed during the communist era in South Yemen.

In 1993, Habib Umar founded Dar al-Mustafa, a traditional Islamic seminary in Tarim that has become one of the most renowned centers of Islamic learning in the modern world. Students from across the globe come to study the traditional Islamic sciences under his guidance and the guidance of other scholars at the institution. The curriculum emphasizes the integration of sacred knowledge with spiritual practice.

He is a Shafi'i jurist, Ash'ari theologian, and a master of the Ba'Alawi Sufi tradition. He travels extensively, delivering lectures and leading gatherings of remembrance (dhikr) in countries across the world. He is known for his emphasis on prophetic character, spiritual purification, and service to the community. He is consistently ranked among the most influential Muslims in the world.

name_full_en: Ali Zayn al-Abidin ibn Abd ar-Rahman al-Jifri
title: Habib
bio_short_en: Yemeni-Emirati Islamic scholar, media personality, and founder of the Tabah Foundation for Islamic studies and research.
bio_en:
Ali Zayn al-Abidin ibn Abd ar-Rahman al-Jifri (b. 1971 CE / 1391 AH), commonly known as Habib Ali al-Jifri, is a prominent Yemeni-Emirati Islamic scholar and public intellectual. Born in Jeddah, Saudi Arabia, to a Hadhrami family from the Ba'Alawi sada, he studied under major scholars in Yemen, including Habib Umar bin Hafiz and Habib Abd al-Qadir al-Saqqaf.

He is the founder and president of the Tabah Foundation for Islamic Studies and Research, based in Abu Dhabi, which promotes scholarly engagement with contemporary issues. He is known for his media presence across the Arab world, delivering accessible lectures on Islamic spirituality, ethics, and contemporary challenges.

Habib Ali is a Shafi'i jurist and a proponent of traditional Sunni scholarship. He has been involved in interfaith dialogue initiatives and is a vocal advocate for moderation and tolerance. He is regularly listed among the most influential Muslims in the world.

title: Sheikh
bio_short_en: Syrian hadith scholar based in Medina, renowned for his critical editions of classical hadith texts and expertise in manuscript studies.
bio_en:
Muhammad ibn Muhammad Awwamah al-Halabi (b. 1940 CE / 1359 AH) is one of the most distinguished living hadith scholars. Born in Aleppo, Syria, he studied under major scholars including Abdul-Fattah Abu Ghuddah, from whom he inherited a deep commitment to hadith sciences and manuscript editing.

Sheikh Awwamah is renowned for his meticulous critical editions of classical Islamic texts. His edition of 'Musannaf Ibn Abi Shaybah' is considered the definitive scholarly edition, and he has produced acclaimed editions of numerous other hadith and fiqh works. His book 'Athar al-Hadith ash-Sharif fi Ikhtilaf al-A'immah al-Fuqaha' (The Effect of Hadith on the Differences Among Jurist-Imams) is a widely studied work that examines why the great imams differed despite using the same hadith sources.

He settled in Medina, where he continues to teach and produce scholarly works. He is a Hanafi jurist with encyclopedic knowledge of the hadith literature across all schools. He represents the continuation of the Aleppine scholarly tradition of rigorous textual criticism and hadith verification.

name_full_en: Abu al-Fadl Abdullah ibn Muhammad ibn as-Siddiq al-Ghumari
title: Sheikh
bio_short_en: Moroccan hadith master and prolific author known for his independent scholarly methodology and expertise in hadith criticism.
bio_en:
Abu al-Fadl Abdullah ibn Muhammad ibn as-Siddiq al-Ghumari (1910-1993 CE / 1328-1413 AH) was a Moroccan hadith scholar of immense erudition and one of the most prolific Islamic authors of the 20th century. Born in Tangier into the illustrious al-Ghumari scholarly family, he studied at al-Qarawiyyin in Fez and al-Azhar in Cairo.

He authored over 200 books and treatises on hadith sciences, fiqh, aqeedah, and various other Islamic disciplines. He was renowned for his independent scholarly approach, not confining himself to a single school of thought in his hadith evaluations while being firmly Maliki in fiqh and Ash'ari in aqeedah. His works on hadith criticism display a rare mastery of the classical hadith evaluation methodology.

Among his notable works are 'al-Mudawi li Ilal al-Jami as-Saghir wa Sharhayn al-Munawi' (a correction of hadith gradings), 'Irgham al-Mubtadi al-Ghabi' (on theological issues), and numerous treatises on specific hadith questions. He engaged in scholarly debates with scholars of various orientations. His family, including his brother Ahmad al-Ghumari, produced one of the most remarkable scholarly lineages of modern Morocco.

name_full_en: Abu al-Fayid Ahmad ibn Muhammad ibn as-Siddiq al-Ghumari
title: Sheikh
bio_short_en: Moroccan hadith master and elder brother of Abdullah al-Ghumari, known for his prodigious memory and vast hadith knowledge.
bio_en:
Abu al-Fayid Ahmad ibn Muhammad ibn as-Siddiq al-Ghumari (1902-1960 CE / 1320-1380 AH) was a Moroccan hadith scholar renowned for his extraordinary memory and vast knowledge of the hadith literature. He was the elder brother of Abdullah al-Ghumari and part of the distinguished al-Ghumari scholarly family of Tangier.

Ahmad al-Ghumari studied under his father Muhammad ibn as-Siddiq al-Ghumari and at al-Qarawiyyin University. He became known for his ability to cite hadiths and their chains of narration from memory with remarkable accuracy. He authored works on hadith sciences and was considered one of the leading hadith authorities of his time in the Maghreb.

His scholarly approach combined deep knowledge of the classical hadith literature with independent critical analysis. He was a Maliki jurist and Ash'ari theologian. His premature death at a relatively young age was considered a great loss to Islamic scholarship, though his scholarly legacy continued through his family members.

title: Sheikh
bio_short_en: Indian Deobandi scholar and author of 'Ma'arif al-Hadith,' a popular hadith compilation widely read in the Indian subcontinent.
bio_en:
Muhammad Manzoor Nomani (1905-1997 CE / 1323-1417 AH) was a prominent Indian Islamic scholar associated with the Deoband school. He studied at Darul Uloom Deoband and became a close associate of Muhammad Ilyas al-Kandhlawi, the founder of the Tablighi Jamaat, though he later distanced himself from the movement.

His most well-known work is 'Ma'arif al-Hadith' (Meanings of Hadith), a multi-volume collection of hadiths with explanations organized by topic. The work is written in Urdu and has been translated into numerous languages. It is one of the most widely read hadith compilations in the Indian subcontinent and beyond, known for its accessibility and practical orientation.

Nomani was also the editor of the Urdu monthly magazine 'al-Furqan,' a leading Islamic journal in India for decades. He authored works criticizing the Iranian Revolution and Khomeinism from a Sunni perspective. He was a Hanafi jurist who emphasized practical Islamic knowledge accessible to the general Muslim public.

name_full_en: Taj ad-Din Abu al-Fadl Ahmad ibn Muhammad ibn Abd al-Karim ibn Ata'illah as-Sakandari
title: Imam
bio_short_en: Shadhili Sufi master and author of 'al-Hikam' (Aphorisms), one of the most celebrated texts of Islamic spirituality.
bio_en:
Taj ad-Din Abu al-Fadl Ahmad ibn Muhammad ibn Ata'illah as-Sakandari (c. 1250-1309 CE / 648-709 AH) was a Maliki jurist, Ash'ari theologian, and the third grand master (qutb) of the Shadhiliyyah Sufi order. Born in Alexandria, Egypt, he initially opposed Sufism before becoming a devoted student of Abu al-Abbas al-Mursi, himself a disciple of Abu al-Hasan ash-Shadhili.

His most famous work, 'al-Hikam al-Ata'iyyah' (The Book of Aphorisms), is one of the most widely read and commented upon texts in Islamic spiritual literature. The 'Hikam' consists of pithy spiritual aphorisms that encapsulate profound truths about the soul's relationship with God. Dozens of scholars have written commentaries on this work over the centuries.

Ibn Ata'illah also authored 'Lata'if al-Minan' (The Subtle Blessings), a biographical account of his Shadhili predecessors, and 'Miftah al-Falah' (The Key to Salvation), a treatise on the practice of dhikr. He taught at al-Azhar Mosque in Cairo and engaged in famous debates with Ibn Taymiyyah on the legitimacy of Sufi practices. His synthesis of legal scholarship, theology, and spirituality made the Shadhiliyyah order one of the most intellectually grounded Sufi traditions.

name_full_en: Abu Abdullah al-Harith ibn Asad al-Muhasabi
title: Imam
bio_short_en: Early Sufi master and theologian known for his works on self-examination (muhasabah) and inner spiritual discipline.
bio_en:
Abu Abdullah al-Harith ibn Asad al-Muhasabi (781-857 CE / 165-243 AH) was one of the earliest and most influential Sufi scholars of Baghdad. His name 'al-Muhasabi' derives from 'muhasabah' (self-examination or self-reckoning), reflecting the central theme of his spiritual teaching. Born in Basra, he settled in Baghdad where he became a leading figure in early Islamic spirituality.

Al-Muhasabi is often considered a bridge between the earliest ascetics (zuhhad) and the later systematic Sufi tradition. His works emphasize rigorous self-examination, sincerity of intention, and the purification of the soul from hidden diseases such as ostentation (riya'), self-admiration (ujb), and worldly attachment. His major work 'ar-Ri'ayah li Huquq Allah' (The Observance of the Rights of God) is a foundational text of Islamic spiritual psychology.

He was a teacher of the great Junaid al-Baghdadi and thus stands at the head of the major chains of Sufi transmission. Despite facing criticism from some Hanbali scholars of his time, his orthodoxy and deep knowledge of the Quran and Sunnah were widely acknowledged. Imam al-Ghazali drew heavily on al-Muhasabi's writings in composing his own 'Ihya Ulum ad-Din.'

name_full_en: Abu al-Fayd Thawban ibn Ibrahim Dhu an-Nun al-Misri
bio_short_en: Early Egyptian Sufi known for articulating the concept of ma'rifah (gnosis) and the stations of the spiritual path.
bio_en:
Abu al-Fayd Thawban ibn Ibrahim, known as Dhu an-Nun al-Misri (796-859 CE / 180-245 AH), was one of the most important early Sufi figures in Islamic history. Born in Akhmim, Upper Egypt, he is credited with being among the first to systematically articulate the Sufi concepts of ma'rifah (direct knowledge of God), ahwal (spiritual states), and maqamat (stations of the spiritual path).

Dhu an-Nun traveled widely in search of knowledge, studying with scholars and ascetics across Egypt, Syria, and Arabia. He was known for his eloquent expressions of divine love and spiritual ecstasy, many of which have been preserved in later Sufi biographical works. He emphasized that true knowledge of God comes not merely through intellectual study but through the purification of the heart and direct spiritual experience.

Despite facing accusations of heresy during his lifetime and being brought before the Abbasid caliph al-Mutawakkil, he was acquitted and recognized for his orthodoxy. He is venerated in the Sufi tradition as one of the pioneers who established the vocabulary and framework of Islamic mysticism. His influence shaped the development of Sufism in Egypt and beyond.

bio_short_en: Pioneering female Sufi saint of Basra, celebrated for her doctrine of pure, selfless love of God.
bio_en:
Rabi'a bint Isma'il al-Adawiyyah (c. 714-801 CE / 95-185 AH) was an early Muslim saint and one of the most celebrated figures in the history of Islamic spirituality. Born into a poor family in Basra, Iraq, she endured great hardship in her early life, including a period of slavery, before devoting herself entirely to the worship and love of God.

Rabi'a is most famous for her articulation of the doctrine of pure, selfless divine love (hubb). She taught that God should be worshipped neither out of fear of Hell nor desire for Paradise, but solely out of love for Him. Her famous prayer captures this: 'O God, if I worship You out of fear of Hell, burn me in Hell. If I worship You in hope of Paradise, exclude me from it. But if I worship You for Your own sake, do not withhold from me Your everlasting beauty.'

Her teachings profoundly influenced the development of the love-centered tradition within Sufism. She refused marriage proposals, dedicating herself exclusively to God. Major Sufi scholars, including Hasan al-Basri (who was her contemporary according to some sources), respected her spiritual station. She passed away in Basra (or Jerusalem, according to some accounts) and remains one of the most revered women saints in Islamic history.

name_full_en: Abu Bakr Muhammad ibn al-Husayn al-Ajurri
title: Imam
bio_short_en: Classical scholar of aqeedah and ethics, author of 'ash-Shari'ah,' a foundational text on Sunni creed.
bio_en:
Abu Bakr Muhammad ibn al-Husayn al-Ajurri (c. 878-970 CE / 264-360 AH) was a prominent scholar of Islamic creed, ethics, and hadith. Born in Baghdad, he studied under numerous scholars and later settled in Mecca, where he spent the last decades of his life teaching and writing.

His most significant work is 'ash-Shari'ah,' a comprehensive treatise on Sunni creed (aqeedah) that presents the orthodox positions on faith, the attributes of God, the status of the Companions, and related theological matters. The work draws extensively on Quran, hadith, and the statements of the early generations (salaf) and has been valued by scholars of various Sunni orientations.

Al-Ajurri also authored 'Akhlaq al-Ulama' (The Ethics of the Scholars), a treatise on the manners and responsibilities of religious scholars, and 'Akhlaq Hamalat al-Quran' (The Ethics of the Bearers of the Quran), on the proper conduct of those who memorize and recite the Quran. He was known for his piety, asceticism, and dedication to preserving traditional Sunni orthodoxy.

name_full_en: Abu al-Qasim Hibatullah ibn al-Hasan al-Lalika'i at-Tabari
title: Imam
bio_short_en: Shafi'i scholar and author of 'Sharh Usul I'tiqad Ahl as-Sunnah,' a foundational compilation on Sunni creed.
bio_en:
Abu al-Qasim Hibatullah ibn al-Hasan al-Lalika'i at-Tabari (971-1027 CE / 360-418 AH) was a Shafi'i jurist, hadith scholar, and author of one of the most important early compilations on Sunni creed. His magnum opus, 'Sharh Usul I'tiqad Ahl as-Sunnah wal-Jama'ah' (Explanation of the Principles of the Creed of Ahl us-Sunnah), compiles narrations from the Quran, hadith, and statements of the early scholars (salaf) on matters of faith.

The work is organized thematically, covering the attributes of God, the nature of the Quran, predestination, the status of the Companions, and other theological topics. It serves as a primary source for understanding the creedal positions of the early Muslim community. Al-Lalika'i collected narrations with their chains of transmission, making the work valuable both as a theological reference and as a hadith collection.

He studied and taught in Baghdad and was respected by scholars of his time for his erudition and piety. His work remains widely referenced by scholars of Sunni theology.

name_full_en: Abu Uthman Isma'il ibn Abdurrahman as-Sabuni an-Naysaburi
title: Imam
bio_short_en: Shafi'i scholar of Nishapur, author of 'Aqidat as-Salaf wa Ashab al-Hadith' on the creed of the early scholars.
bio_en:
Abu Uthman Isma'il ibn Abdurrahman as-Sabuni (983-1057 CE / 373-449 AH) was a prominent Shafi'i scholar, preacher, and hadith master of Nishapur. He served as the preacher (khatib) of Nishapur and was one of the leading religious figures of Khurasan in his time.

His best-known work is 'Aqidat as-Salaf wa Ashab al-Hadith' (The Creed of the Early Scholars and the People of Hadith), a concise but authoritative treatise on Sunni orthodox theology as understood by the scholars of hadith. The work presents the creedal positions of the salaf on key theological questions and has been valued by scholars across centuries.

As-Sabuni was also known for his eloquent preaching and his role in the intellectual life of Nishapur during its golden age as a center of Islamic learning. He was a contemporary of major scholars such as al-Bayhaqi and al-Qushayri.

name_full_en: Abu Umar Yusuf ibn Abdullah ibn Muhammad ibn Abd al-Barr an-Namari
title: Imam
bio_short_en: Andalusian Maliki jurist and hadith master, author of 'at-Tamhid' and 'al-Istidhkar,' major commentaries on the Muwatta of Imam Malik.
bio_en:
Abu Umar Yusuf ibn Abdullah ibn Abd al-Barr an-Namari (978-1071 CE / 368-463 AH) was one of the greatest Maliki jurists and hadith scholars of al-Andalus. Born in Cordoba, he studied under numerous Andalusian scholars and became the leading authority on hadith and Maliki jurisprudence in the Islamic West.

His two most important works are commentaries on the Muwatta of Imam Malik: 'at-Tamhid lima fi al-Muwatta min al-Ma'ani wal-Asanid' (a comprehensive commentary focusing on hadith analysis) and 'al-Istidhkar' (a commentary focusing on jurisprudence and the positions of the different schools). Both works are massive encyclopedias of hadith and fiqh that remain essential references.

He also authored 'Jami Bayan al-Ilm wa Fadlih' (A Compendium on the Merit of Knowledge), one of the most beloved works on the virtues and etiquette of seeking knowledge, and 'al-Isti'ab fi Ma'rifat al-Ashab,' a major biographical dictionary of the Companions of the Prophet. Ibn Abd al-Barr was known for his breadth of learning and his fair treatment of the positions of all four Sunni schools of law.

name_full_en: Abu Amr Uthman ibn Abdurrahman ash-Shahrazuri (Ibn as-Salah)
title: Imam
bio_short_en: Author of the 'Muqaddimah' on hadith sciences, the foundational textbook that shaped all subsequent hadith methodology.
bio_en:
