Abu Amr Uthman ibn Abdurrahman ash-Shahrazuri (1181-1245 CE / 577-643 AH), known as Ibn as-Salah, was a Shafi'i jurist and one of the most influential hadith methodologists in Islamic history. Born in Shahrazur (modern Iraqi Kurdistan), he studied in Mosul, Baghdad, and the Levant before settling in Damascus.

His most famous work, commonly known as the 'Muqaddimah of Ibn as-Salah' (Ulum al-Hadith), revolutionized the science of hadith methodology. Building on the works of al-Khatib al-Baghdadi and earlier scholars, he produced the most comprehensive and systematic treatment of hadith terminology and evaluation that had yet been written. The work became the standard textbook on hadith methodology and was the basis for virtually every subsequent work in the field, including those by an-Nawawi, Ibn Hajar, and as-Suyuti.

Ibn as-Salah taught at the Dar al-Hadith al-Ashrafiyyah in Damascus, one of the most prestigious hadith institutions of the medieval period. He also authored a commentary on the 'Wasit' of al-Ghazali in Shafi'i fiqh. His methodological classifications of hadith remain the standard framework used by hadith scholars to this day.

name_full_en: Abu Bakr Abdullah ibn Muhammad ibn Abi ad-Dunya
title: Imam
bio_short_en: Prolific Baghdadi scholar who authored hundreds of works on Islamic ethics, asceticism, and the afterlife.
bio_en:
Abu Bakr Abdullah ibn Muhammad ibn Abi ad-Dunya (823-894 CE / 208-281 AH) was one of the most prolific scholars of early Islam. Born in Baghdad, he served as a tutor to the sons of the Abbasid caliphs and was a leading figure in Baghdadi scholarly circles.

Ibn Abi ad-Dunya authored an enormous number of works, with estimates ranging from 200 to over 300 titles. His writings primarily focus on Islamic ethics (adab), asceticism (zuhd), spiritual virtues, and eschatology (the afterlife, the grave, and the Day of Judgment). His works collect hadith narrations and early Muslim sayings (athar) organized by theme, creating an invaluable treasury of early Islamic moral and spiritual literature.

Among his notable works are 'Kitab al-Qubur' (The Book of Graves), 'Kitab at-Tawba' (The Book of Repentance), 'Kitab as-Samt' (The Book of Silence), 'Dhamm ad-Dunya' (The Censure of the World), and 'al-Ikhlas wan-Niyyah' (Sincerity and Intention). His collections provide a unique window into the spiritual and ethical concerns of the early Muslim community and remain widely referenced by scholars and preachers.

name_full_en: Abu al-Fayd Muhammad Murtada al-Husayni az-Zabidi
title: Imam
bio_short_en: Indian-born scholar who settled in Cairo, author of 'Taj al-Arus,' the largest Arabic dictionary, and 'Ithaf as-Sadah,' a commentary on al-Ghazali's Ihya.
bio_en:
Abu al-Fayd Muhammad Murtada al-Husayni az-Zabidi (1732-1790 CE / 1145-1205 AH) was a polymath scholar of Indian origin who became one of the leading intellectual figures of 18th-century Cairo. Born in Bilgram, India, he traveled to Yemen (where he acquired the nisba 'az-Zabidi' from the city of Zabid), the Hijaz, and finally settled in Cairo, where he spent the rest of his life.

His two magnum opuses are monumental works of scholarship. 'Taj al-Arus min Jawahir al-Qamus' (The Crown of the Bride from the Jewels of the Qamus) is the largest dictionary of the Arabic language ever compiled, expanding the earlier 'Qamus al-Muhit' of al-Fayruzabadi into a massive encyclopedia of Arabic lexicography. 'Ithaf as-Sadah al-Muttaqin bi Sharh Ihya Ulum ad-Din' (The Gift to the God-Fearing Masters) is a comprehensive commentary on al-Ghazali's 'Ihya Ulum ad-Din,' adding hadith verification and additional scholarly analysis.

Az-Zabidi was a Hanafi jurist, hadith scholar, linguist, and Naqshbandi Sufi. He held teaching sessions attended by scholars from across the Muslim world and received patronage from Ottoman officials. His scholarly output demonstrated an encyclopedic mastery of Arabic language, hadith, fiqh, and tasawwuf.

name_full_en: Shihab ad-Din Abu Shamah Abd ar-Rahman ibn Isma'il al-Maqdisi
title: Imam
bio_short_en: Shafi'i historian and scholar of Damascus, teacher of an-Nawawi, author of 'ar-Rawdatayn' on the Crusades era.
bio_en:
Shihab ad-Din Abu Shamah Abd ar-Rahman ibn Isma'il al-Maqdisi (1203-1267 CE / 599-665 AH) was a distinguished Shafi'i scholar, historian, and teacher at the Dar al-Hadith al-Ashrafiyyah in Damascus. He is best known as one of the teachers of Imam an-Nawawi.

His most significant historical work is 'ar-Rawdatayn fi Akhbar ad-Dawlatayn an-Nuriyyah was-Salahiyyah' (The Two Gardens: On the History of the Nurid and Ayyubid Dynasties), a major chronicle covering the careers of Nur ad-Din Zangi and Salah ad-Din al-Ayyubi and the Crusades era. He also wrote on Quranic sciences, grammar, and Islamic law.

Abu Shamah was an advocate of reform and criticized certain innovations he observed in his society. He emphasized the importance of following the Quran and Sunnah and wrote 'al-Ba'ith ala Inkar al-Bida' wal-Hawadith' (The Motive for Denouncing Innovations), which discusses the distinction between praiseworthy and blameworthy innovations.

name_full_en: Zakiyy ad-Din Abu Muhammad Abd al-Azim ibn Abd al-Qawi al-Mundhiri
title: Imam
bio_short_en: Egyptian hadith master and author of 'at-Targhib wat-Tarhib,' a celebrated collection of hadiths on encouragement and warning.
bio_en:
Zakiyy ad-Din Abu Muhammad Abd al-Azim al-Mundhiri (1185-1258 CE / 581-656 AH) was one of the leading hadith scholars of Egypt. Born in Gharbiyyah, Egypt, he studied under numerous scholars in Egypt, the Hijaz, and the Levant, and became the head of the Dar al-Hadith al-Kamiliyyah in Cairo.

His most famous work, 'at-Targhib wat-Tarhib min al-Hadith ash-Sharif' (Encouragement and Warning from the Prophetic Hadith), is a widely studied compilation of hadiths organized by themes of virtues and vices. The work has been used by preachers, teachers, and students for centuries as a practical guide to Islamic ethics. He also wrote 'Mukhtasar Sahih Muslim' (an abridgement of Sahih Muslim) and 'Mukhtasar Sunan Abi Dawud.'

Al-Mundhiri was a Shafi'i jurist known for his meticulous approach to hadith grading. His assessments of hadith authenticity are regularly cited in later scholarly works. He served in leading scholarly positions in Cairo during the Ayyubid period and trained many of the next generation's hadith scholars.

name_full_en: Nasr ibn Muhammad ibn Ahmad Abu al-Layth as-Samarqandi
title: Imam
bio_short_en: Hanafi jurist and mufassir from Samarqand, known as the 'Imam of Guidance' for his works on practical piety and Quranic exegesis.
bio_en:
Nasr ibn Muhammad ibn Ahmad Abu al-Layth as-Samarqandi (c. 898-983 CE / 286-373 AH) was a major Hanafi jurist, mufassir, and author from Samarqand (in modern Uzbekistan). Known by the honorific 'Imam al-Huda' (Imam of Guidance), he was one of the most influential scholars of Central Asia.

His Quran commentary, 'Bahr al-Ulum' (The Ocean of Sciences), is a comprehensive tafsir that combines linguistic analysis, legal rulings, and spiritual insights. It was widely studied in the Hanafi scholarly tradition. He also authored 'Tanbih al-Ghafilin' (Alerting the Heedless), a popular work on Islamic ethics and spiritual admonitions that became one of the most widely read books of practical piety in the Muslim world.

As-Samarqandi wrote extensively on Hanafi fiqh, including 'Nawazil' (Legal Queries), a collection of legal opinions that reflects the practical jurisprudence of Central Asian Muslim communities. His works bridged the gap between scholarly discourse and popular understanding, making advanced Islamic knowledge accessible to broader audiences.

name_full_en: Abu Abdullah Muhammad ibn Ali al-Hakim at-Tirmidhi
bio_short_en: Early Sufi scholar and author of 'Khatm al-Awliya' (The Seal of the Saints), a pioneering work on the concept of sainthood in Islam.
bio_en:
Abu Abdullah Muhammad ibn Ali al-Hakim at-Tirmidhi (c. 820-910 CE / 205-298 AH) was one of the most original thinkers in early Islamic spirituality. Not to be confused with the hadith compiler Imam at-Tirmidhi, al-Hakim at-Tirmidhi was born in Tirmidh (modern Uzbekistan) and was a scholar of hadith, fiqh, and Sufi metaphysics.

His most influential work is 'Khatm al-Awliya' (The Seal of the Saints), which developed a sophisticated theory of sainthood (wilayah) in Islam, paralleling the concept of prophethood. He theorized about the spiritual hierarchy and the roles of the saints in the divine economy. This concept of the 'Seal of Saints' influenced later Sufi thinkers, most notably Ibn Arabi.

He also authored 'Nawadir al-Usul fi Ma'rifat Ahadith ar-Rasul' (Rare Principles in Understanding Prophetic Hadith), which combines hadith commentary with spiritual insights. His thought represents an important stage in the development of Islamic mystical philosophy, bridging the earlier ascetic tradition with the more systematic Sufi metaphysics that followed.

name_full_en: Shihab ad-Din Abu al-Abbas Ahmad ibn Ahmad Zarruq al-Fasi
title: Imam
bio_short_en: Moroccan Maliki jurist and Sufi reformer, author of 'Qawa'id at-Tasawwuf' (Principles of Sufism) and commentaries on the Hikam.
bio_en:
Shihab ad-Din Abu al-Abbas Ahmad ibn Ahmad Zarruq (1442-1494 CE / 846-899 AH) was a Moroccan Maliki jurist, hadith scholar, and Sufi master known for his efforts to align Sufi practice with Islamic law. Born in Fez, Morocco, he studied under scholars in Fez, Egypt, and the Hijaz.

His most celebrated work is 'Qawa'id at-Tasawwuf' (The Principles of Sufism), which presents 225 rules for Sufi practice grounded in Sharia compliance. He also wrote numerous commentaries on the 'Hikam' of Ibn Ata'illah, producing what are considered among the most authoritative explanations of that classic text. Scholars note that he wrote at least seventeen different commentaries on the Hikam.

Zarruq was a reformer who critiqued deviant Sufi practices while defending the legitimacy of orthodox Sufism. He founded the Zarruqiyyah branch of the Shadhiliyyah order. His approach, insisting on the inseparability of outward law and inward spirituality, influenced Sufi practice across North and West Africa for centuries.

name_full_en: Abu Muhammad Abd al-Wahid ibn Ahmad ibn Ali ibn Ashir al-Ansari
title: Imam
bio_short_en: Moroccan Maliki scholar, author of 'al-Murshid al-Mu'in,' a widely memorized didactic poem on fiqh, aqeedah, and tasawwuf.
bio_en:
Abu Muhammad Abd al-Wahid ibn Ahmad ibn Ashir al-Ansari (1582-1631 CE / 990-1040 AH) was a Moroccan Maliki scholar from Fez known primarily for his celebrated didactic poem 'al-Murshid al-Mu'in ala ad-Daruri min Ulum ad-Din' (The Helpful Guide to the Essential Religious Sciences).

The 'Murshid al-Mu'in' is a 317-verse poem that covers the fundamentals of Maliki jurisprudence, Ash'ari theology, and Junaidi Sufi ethics in a concise and elegant format. The poem became the standard introductory text in religious education across Morocco, West Africa, and other Maliki-majority regions. It has been memorized by countless students and has attracted numerous commentaries over the centuries.

Ibn Ashir's genius was in synthesizing the three essential Islamic sciences (fiqh, aqeedah, and tasawwuf) into a single accessible work. He was a Maliki jurist, Ash'ari theologian, and Shadhili Sufi who represented the mainstream scholarly tradition of Morocco. He taught at the Qarawiyyin Mosque in Fez.

name_full_en: Abu Zaid Abdurrahman ibn Muhammad al-Akhdari
bio_short_en: Algerian Maliki scholar, author of 'al-Sullam al-Munawraq' on logic and 'Mukhtasar al-Akhdari' on fiqh, both widely studied across Africa.
bio_en:
Abu Zaid Abdurrahman ibn Muhammad al-Akhdari (1512-1575 CE / 918-983 AH) was an Algerian Maliki scholar from the oasis town of Biskra. Despite his relatively short life, he produced works that became cornerstones of Islamic education across North and West Africa.

His 'al-Sullam al-Munawraq fi Ilm al-Mantiq' (The Illuminated Ladder in the Science of Logic) is a versified treatise on formal logic that has been a standard textbook in traditional Islamic curricula for centuries. His 'Mukhtasar al-Akhdari' is a concise manual of Maliki fiqh covering purification, prayer, and related topics that served as a first introduction to Islamic law for generations of students.

Both works are characterized by clarity, precision, and pedagogical effectiveness. They attracted numerous commentaries and super-commentaries, reflecting their central role in the Islamic educational tradition. Al-Akhdari's influence on the intellectual formation of Muslim scholars in Africa has been immense.

name_full_en: Abu Abdullah Muhammad ibn Yusuf as-Sanusi at-Tilimsani
title: Imam
bio_short_en: Algerian Ash'ari theologian, author of 'Umm al-Barahin,' the most widely studied creed text in North and West Africa.
bio_en:
Abu Abdullah Muhammad ibn Yusuf as-Sanusi at-Tilimsani (1428-1490 CE / 832-895 AH) was a prominent Algerian Ash'ari theologian and Maliki jurist from Tlemcen. He is the author of the most widely studied texts on Islamic theology in the Maliki world.

His masterwork, known as 'Umm al-Barahin' (The Mother of Proofs) or 'al-Aqidah as-Sughra,' is a concise creed text that presents the attributes of God, the attributes of the prophets, and the rationally necessary, impossible, and possible attributes of each. The work's systematic approach made it the standard introductory theology text across North Africa, West Africa, and the Malay world. He also wrote a medium-length creed ('al-Aqidah al-Wusta') and an extended one ('al-Aqidah al-Kubra').

As-Sanusi was a rigorous Ash'ari theologian who also had Sufi inclinations. His works on theology remain the entry point for students of Islamic creed in Maliki-majority regions and have attracted numerous commentaries. His influence on Islamic theological education in Africa is comparable to that of an-Nawawi's 'Arba'in' (Forty Hadith) on hadith education globally.

name_full_en: Diya' ad-Din Khalil ibn Ishaq ibn Musa al-Jundi
title: Sheikh
bio_short_en: Egyptian Maliki jurist, author of the 'Mukhtasar,' the most authoritative reference of Maliki fiqh for centuries.
bio_en:
Diya' ad-Din Khalil ibn Ishaq al-Jundi (c. 1320-1374 CE / 720-776 AH) was an Egyptian Maliki jurist whose 'Mukhtasar' (Abridgement) became the single most authoritative and studied text in the Maliki school of jurisprudence. The work is an extremely concise summary of Maliki law that covers the entire range of legal topics.

Despite its brevity, the 'Mukhtasar of Khalil' encodes an enormous amount of legal content using a highly technical terminology that requires extensive commentary to fully unpack. It attracted dozens of commentaries, the most famous being those by al-Kharashi, ad-Dardir, and ad-Dusuqi. For centuries, it served as the foundational text in Maliki legal education from Egypt to West Africa.

Khalil was a professor at the Shaykhuniyyah Madrasa in Cairo and was known for his mastery of Maliki jurisprudence. His 'Mukhtasar' represents the culmination of the Maliki tradition of legal abridgement and remains the primary reference for legal rulings in the Maliki school.

name_full_en: Abu al-Qasim Muhammad ibn Ahmad ibn Juzayy al-Kalbi al-Gharnati
bio_short_en: Andalusian Maliki scholar and author of 'at-Tashil li Ulum at-Tanzil,' a popular Quran commentary, and 'al-Qawanin al-Fiqhiyyah' on comparative fiqh.
bio_en:
Abu al-Qasim Muhammad ibn Ahmad ibn Juzayy al-Kalbi al-Gharnati (1294-1340 CE / 693-741 AH) was an Andalusian Maliki scholar from Granada who produced important works in Quranic exegesis and comparative jurisprudence. He came from a distinguished scholarly family in Granada.

His tafsir, 'at-Tashil li Ulum at-Tanzil' (The Facilitation of the Sciences of Revelation), is a clear and accessible Quran commentary that draws on the major earlier tafsirs while presenting the material in a concise, student-friendly format. His 'al-Qawanin al-Fiqhiyyah' (The Legal Codes) is a unique work of comparative fiqh that presents the positions of all four Sunni schools on each legal issue, making it an invaluable reference for understanding the breadth of Islamic legal opinion.

Ibn Juzayy died as a martyr at the Battle of Tarifa in 1340, fighting alongside the Marinid forces against the Christian kingdoms of Iberia. His scholarly legacy, particularly his accessible approach to complex Islamic sciences, has ensured that his works continue to be studied across the Muslim world.

name_full_en: Athir ad-Din Abu Hayyan Muhammad ibn Yusuf al-Andalusi
title: Imam
bio_short_en: Andalusian-Egyptian grammarian and mufassir, author of 'al-Bahr al-Muhit,' a major Quran commentary focused on Arabic grammar.
bio_en:
Athir ad-Din Abu Hayyan Muhammad ibn Yusuf al-Andalusi (1256-1344 CE / 654-745 AH) was one of the greatest Arabic grammarians and Quran commentators of the medieval period. Born in Granada, al-Andalus, he emigrated to Egypt where he became the leading grammarian of Cairo and taught at al-Azhar.

His monumental tafsir, 'al-Bahr al-Muhit' (The Encompassing Sea), is one of the most important Quran commentaries focused on grammatical and linguistic analysis. The work demonstrates mastery of Arabic grammar, rhetoric, and the various Quranic recitations (qira'at), making it an essential reference for scholars of Arabic language and Quranic sciences.

Abu Hayyan also authored works on Arabic grammar, including a commentary on the Alfiyyah of Ibn Malik, and was known for his independent grammatical views that sometimes challenged established authorities. He was a Zahiri in fiqh but taught across all schools. His command of multiple languages, including Turkish and Ethiopian, was noted by his contemporaries.

name_full_en: Adud ad-Din Abd ar-Rahman ibn Ahmad al-Iji
title: Qadi
bio_short_en: Ash'ari theologian and Shafi'i judge, author of 'al-Mawaqif,' one of the most comprehensive treatises on Islamic theology.
bio_en:
Adud ad-Din Abd ar-Rahman ibn Ahmad al-Iji (1281-1355 CE / 680-756 AH) was a major Ash'ari theologian and Shafi'i jurist from Ij (in modern Fars province, Iran). He served as a judge (qadi) in the Ilkhanid and later Muzaffarid domains.

His most influential work, 'al-Mawaqif fi Ilm al-Kalam' (Stations in the Science of Theology), is one of the most comprehensive and sophisticated treatises on Ash'ari theology. It covers epistemology, ontology, the nature of God and His attributes, prophethood, eschatology, and the imamate. The work attracted the famous commentary of al-Jurjani ('Sharh al-Mawaqif'), and together the two works became the standard advanced theology textbook in Islamic madrasas for centuries.

Al-Iji also authored 'al-Fawa'id al-Ghiyathiyyah' on Arabic rhetoric, reflecting his broad scholarly interests. He died in prison after political conflicts. His theological system represents the mature phase of Ash'ari kalam, integrating philosophical methods with traditional theological concerns.

name_full_en: Sa'd ad-Din Mas'ud ibn Umar at-Taftazani
title: Imam
bio_short_en: Central Asian polymath, author of 'Sharh al-Aqaid an-Nasafiyyah' on Maturidi theology and 'al-Mutawwal' on Arabic rhetoric.
bio_en:
Sa'd ad-Din Mas'ud ibn Umar at-Taftazani (1322-1390 CE / 722-792 AH) was one of the most brilliant and versatile scholars of the late medieval period. Born in Taftazan (modern Turkmenistan), he displayed prodigious scholarly talent from a young age and became a master of theology, logic, rhetoric, grammar, and jurisprudence.

His commentary 'Sharh al-Aqaid an-Nasafiyyah' (Commentary on the Nasafi Creed) became the standard theology textbook in Hanafi madrasas worldwide and remains so in many institutions today. The work expounds the Maturidi theological positions of Najm ad-Din an-Nasafi with clarity and rigor. His 'al-Mutawwal' (on rhetoric) and 'Sharh at-Talwih' (on usul al-fiqh) are equally celebrated in their respective fields.

At-Taftazani lived during the era of Timur (Tamerlane) and spent time at Timur's court in Samarqand. He engaged in famous scholarly debates with al-Jurjani, another leading scholar of the era. His works are characterized by their systematic organization, intellectual depth, and accessibility, which is why they became standard textbooks across the Muslim world.

name_full_en: Ali ibn Muhammad as-Sayyid ash-Sharif al-Jurjani
bio_short_en: Theologian and polymath, author of 'Sharh al-Mawaqif' and 'Kitab at-Ta'rifat,' an essential dictionary of Islamic scholarly terminology.
bio_en:
Ali ibn Muhammad as-Sayyid ash-Sharif al-Jurjani (1339-1413 CE / 740-816 AH) was a major Ash'ari theologian, Hanafi jurist, and encyclopedic scholar. Born in Jurjan (Gorgan, modern Iran), he studied in Herat, Kirman, Egypt, and the Ottoman lands before settling in Shiraz, where he became the leading scholar of his era.

His most famous works include 'Sharh al-Mawaqif' (Commentary on al-Iji's al-Mawaqif), which became the definitive text on advanced Ash'ari theology, and 'Kitab at-Ta'rifat' (The Book of Definitions), an invaluable dictionary defining technical terms used across the Islamic sciences. The 'Ta'rifat' remains a standard reference for scholars, students, and translators.

Al-Jurjani was a contemporary and scholarly rival of at-Taftazani. He wrote on theology, logic, philosophy, Arabic grammar, rhetoric, and jurisprudence. His encyclopedic output and the clarity of his expositions ensured that his works became foundational texts in Islamic higher education across the Persian and Ottoman scholarly traditions.

name_full_en: Abd al-Ali Muhammad ibn Nizam ad-Din al-Birjandi
bio_short_en: Persian astronomer and mathematician who wrote on the rotation of the Earth and produced influential works on mathematical astronomy.
bio_en:
Abd al-Ali Muhammad ibn Nizam ad-Din al-Birjandi (c. 1450-1528 CE / 854-934 AH) was a Persian astronomer, mathematician, and scholar from Birjand in Khurasan. He was one of the most important scientists of the Timurid and early Safavid periods.

Al-Birjandi is notable for his early discussion of the Earth's rotation, presenting arguments about whether the Earth rotates on its axis. His commentary on Nasir ad-Din at-Tusi's astronomical works and his own treatises on mathematical astronomy demonstrate sophisticated scientific reasoning. He also wrote on mathematics, geography, and the astrolabe.

He taught and wrote at a time when the Islamic scientific tradition was producing its last great works before the dominance of European science. His writings on astronomy and mathematics were studied in Persian-speaking lands for generations.

name_full_en: Siraj ad-Din Abu Hafs Umar ibn Ali ibn al-Mulaqqin
title: Imam
bio_short_en: Prolific Egyptian Shafi'i jurist and hadith scholar, one of the most productive authors in Islamic history.
bio_en:
Siraj ad-Din Abu Hafs Umar ibn Ali ibn al-Mulaqqin (1323-1401 CE / 723-804 AH) was an Egyptian Shafi'i jurist and hadith scholar who ranks among the most prolific authors in Islamic history. Born in Cairo, he studied under the leading scholars of his time and became a respected authority in both fiqh and hadith.

Ibn al-Mulaqqin authored approximately 300 works, covering hadith commentary, Shafi'i jurisprudence, biographical literature, and other fields. His 'al-Badr al-Munir fi Takhrij al-Ahadith wal-Athar al-Waqi'ah fi ash-Sharh al-Kabir' is a massive work verifying the hadith citations in ar-Rafi'i's commentary on al-Ghazali's legal writings. He also wrote 'at-Tawdih li Sharh al-Jami as-Sahih,' a commentary on Sahih al-Bukhari.

Contemporary to Ibn Hajar al-Asqalani's teacher (they overlapped in time), Ibn al-Mulaqqin represents the extraordinary scholarly productivity of the Mamluk era in Cairo. His works provide essential source verification for the hadith cited in Shafi'i legal literature.

name_full_en: Nur ad-Din Ali ibn Abi Bakr al-Haythami
title: Imam
bio_short_en: Egyptian hadith scholar, author of 'Majma az-Zawa'id,' a comprehensive compilation of hadiths from major collections not in the Six Books.
bio_en:
Nur ad-Din Ali ibn Abi Bakr al-Haythami (1335-1405 CE / 735-807 AH) was an Egyptian hadith scholar best known for his encyclopedic hadith compilation 'Majma' az-Zawa'id wa Manba' al-Fawa'id' (The Collection of Additional Sources and the Spring of Benefits).

This work gathers hadiths from the Musnads of Ahmad ibn Hanbal, Abu Ya'la, and al-Bazzar, as well as the Mu'jams of at-Tabarani, that are not found in the six canonical hadith collections (Bukhari, Muslim, Abu Dawud, Tirmidhi, Nasai, and Ibn Majah). For each hadith, al-Haythami evaluates the chain of narration, identifying weak and reliable narrators. The work became an indispensable tool for hadith scholars seeking narrations beyond the standard collections.

Al-Haythami was a close student and companion of Zayn ad-Din al-Iraqi, one of the great hadith masters of the era. His compilation methodology and narrator evaluations continue to be cited in contemporary hadith scholarship.

name_full_en: Muhammad ibn Ibrahim ibn al-Wazir al-Yamani
title: Imam
bio_short_en: Yemeni scholar known for advocating return to the Quran and Sunnah, author of 'al-Awasim wal-Qawasim fi adh-Dhabb an Sunnat Abi al-Qasim.'
bio_en:
Muhammad ibn Ibrahim ibn al-Wazir al-Yamani (1370-1436 CE / 775-840 AH) was a Yemeni scholar from the prestigious Banu al-Wazir family, known for his independent scholarly approach and his call to return to the Quran and Sunnah as the primary sources of Islamic law.

Born into a Zaydi family in Yemen, Ibn al-Wazir critically engaged with the Zaydi legal tradition while drawing on Sunni hadith methodology. His most important work, 'al-Awasim wal-Qawasim fi adh-Dhabb an Sunnat Abi al-Qasim' (The Safeguards and the Decisive Arguments in Defense of the Sunnah of Abu al-Qasim), is a defense of Sunni hadith methodology and the reliability of the Companions as narrators.

He also authored 'Ithar al-Haqq ala al-Khalq' (Preferring Truth over Creation), which advocates for following evidence over blind adherence to any particular school. His scholarly independence and emphasis on hadith-based jurisprudence made him an important precursor to later reform movements in Yemen.

bio_short_en: Damascene scholar and author of 'Subul al-Huda war-Rashad,' one of the most comprehensive books on the Prophetic biography (sirah).
bio_en:
Muhammad ibn Yusuf as-Salihi ash-Shami (1460-1535 CE / 864-942 AH) was a Damascene Shafi'i scholar best known for his monumental work on the Prophetic biography, 'Subul al-Huda war-Rashad fi Sirat Khayr al-Ibad' (The Paths of Guidance in the Biography of the Best of Mankind).

This encyclopedic work, spanning approximately twelve volumes, is one of the most comprehensive compilations on the life of Prophet Muhammad ever written. It gathers material from hundreds of earlier sources and organizes it thematically, covering every aspect of the Prophet's life, characteristics, miracles, wives, children, servants, and companions.

As-Salihi drew on the works of earlier seerah scholars such as Ibn Ishaq, Ibn Hisham, Ibn Kathir, and many others, while adding his own analysis and verification. The work became an important reference for scholars of sirah and hadith. He also authored other works on various Islamic sciences.

title: Sheikh
bio_short_en: Damascene Hanafi jurist, Sufi master, and prolific author of over 500 works on Islamic sciences, literature, and travel.
bio_en:
Abd al-Ghani ibn Isma'il an-Nabulusi (1641-1731 CE / 1050-1143 AH) was one of the most prolific and versatile scholars of the Ottoman period. Born in Damascus to a family originally from Nablus, Palestine, he became one of the leading intellectual figures of the 17th-18th century Muslim world.

An-Nabulusi authored over 500 works on an extraordinary range of subjects: Hanafi jurisprudence, Quran commentary, hadith, theology, Sufism, philosophy, poetry, travel literature, and agriculture. He was a Hanafi jurist, Naqshbandi and Qadiri Sufi, and an independent thinker who engaged deeply with the works of Ibn Arabi and Sufi metaphysics.

His travel accounts, particularly 'al-Haqiqah wal-Majaz fi Rihlat Bilad ash-Sham wa Misr wal-Hijaz,' provide valuable descriptions of 17th-century Ottoman Palestine, Syria, and Egypt. He was a central figure in the intellectual and spiritual life of Damascus during the Ottoman era and his works demonstrate the continued vitality of Islamic scholarship during this period.

title: Sheikh
bio_short_en: Syrian scholar from a distinguished scholarly family, known for his traditional Islamic teaching and work in counter-extremism.
bio_en:
Muhammad Abu al-Huda al-Yaqoubi (b. 1963 CE / 1383 AH) is a Syrian Islamic scholar from one of the most prominent scholarly families of Damascus. His father, Ibrahim al-Yaqoubi, was a leading scholar and Sufi master, and the family's scholarly lineage extends back many generations.

Sheikh al-Yaqoubi studied under his father and other major scholars of Damascus, mastering the traditional Islamic sciences of jurisprudence, hadith, theology, and Arabic language. He is a Shafi'i and Hanafi jurist with deep knowledge of the Ash'ari and Maturidi theological traditions. He taught at the Grand Umayyad Mosque in Damascus before leaving Syria during the civil war.

He is recognized internationally for his work in counter-extremism, having issued detailed refutations of ISIS ideology based on traditional Islamic scholarship. His works include treatises on Islamic creed, Prophetic biography, and spiritual purification. He was included in the Royal Islamic Strategic Studies Centre's list of the 500 Most Influential Muslims.

title: Sheikh
bio_short_en: American Muslim scholar, co-founder of Zaytuna College, widely regarded as one of the most influential Muslims in the Western world.
bio_en:
Mark Hanson, known as Hamza Yusuf (b. 1958 CE / 1378 AH), is an American Muslim scholar, educator, and one of the most prominent Muslim intellectuals in the English-speaking world. Born in Walla Walla, Washington, he converted to Islam at age 17 and subsequently spent over ten years studying the traditional Islamic sciences in the Muslim world, including in the UAE, Saudi Arabia, and Mauritania.

He co-founded Zaytuna College in Berkeley, California, the first accredited Muslim liberal arts college in the United States. Zaytuna's curriculum integrates the classical Islamic sciences with the Western liberal arts tradition, representing a vision of Islamic education that engages with modern Western intellectual life.

Sheikh Hamza Yusuf is a Maliki jurist who has studied deeply in the Ash'ari theological and Sufi spiritual traditions. He is known for his eloquent English lectures on Islamic theology, ethics, and civilization, and has authored translations of classical Islamic texts. He has been consistently named among the most influential Muslims in the world and has been involved in interfaith dialogue and public policy discussions.

bio_short_en: Egyptian Islamic activist and author, founder of the Muslim Women's Association and author of 'Return of the Pharaoh' about her imprisonment.
bio_en:
Zainab al-Ghazali al-Jubaili (1917-2005 CE / 1335-1426 AH) was a prominent Egyptian Islamic activist, preacher, and author. She founded the Muslim Women's Association (Jama'at as-Sayyidat al-Muslimat) in 1936, which grew to have millions of members before being dissolved by the Egyptian government.

She played a significant role in Islamic activism in Egypt and was imprisoned under the Nasser regime from 1965 to 1971, during which she endured severe torture. Her memoir 'Ayyam min Hayati' (Days from My Life, published in English as 'Return of the Pharaoh') documents her experience of imprisonment and remains an important account of the persecution of Islamic activists in 20th-century Egypt.

Al-Ghazali was known for her powerful lectures and writings on the role of women in Islam, Islamic family values, and the importance of Islamic education. She authored a Quran commentary titled 'Nazarat fi Kitab Allah' (Reflections on the Book of God) and numerous other works on Islamic thought and activism.

name_full_en: Abu Abdullah Muhammad ibn Tumart al-Masmudi
bio_short_en: Berber religious reformer and founder of the Almohad movement in the Maghreb, known for his emphasis on divine unity (tawhid).
bio_en:
Abu Abdullah Muhammad ibn Tumart (c. 1080-1130 CE / 473-524 AH) was a Berber religious scholar and reformer from the Atlas Mountains of Morocco who founded the Almohad (al-Muwahhidun) movement. He studied in Baghdad, Egypt, and the Hijaz, where he was influenced by al-Ghazali's works and Ash'ari theology.

Returning to the Maghreb, he began a campaign of religious reform, calling for strict adherence to tawhid (the oneness of God) and criticizing what he saw as anthropomorphic beliefs among the ruling Almoravids. His movement attracted a large Berber following, and though he died before achieving political victory, his followers went on to establish the Almohad Caliphate, which ruled the Maghreb and al-Andalus from 1121 to 1269.

Ibn Tumart's theological positions, which combined elements of Ash'ari thought with his own interpretations, were influential in North African Islam. While his claim to be the Mahdi and some of his methods were controversial, his emphasis on doctrinal reform and the primacy of tawhid had a lasting impact on the religious and political history of the Islamic West.

name_full_en: Izz ad-Din Abd al-Aziz ibn Abd as-Salam ibn Abi al-Qasim as-Sulami
title: Sultan al-Ulama
bio_short_en: Known as 'Sultan al-Ulama' (Sultan of the Scholars), a Shafi'i jurist renowned for his courage in confronting unjust rulers and his masterwork 'Qawa'id al-Ahkam.'
bio_en:
Izz ad-Din Abd al-Aziz ibn Abd as-Salam (1181-1262 CE / 577-660 AH), known as Sultan al-Ulama (the Sultan of the Scholars), was one of the most towering figures in Islamic jurisprudence. Born in Damascus, he became the leading Shafi'i jurist of his time and was renowned for his fearless advocacy of justice and truth before political authorities.

His magnum opus, 'Qawa'id al-Ahkam fi Masalih al-Anam' (The Principles of Rulings in the Interests of Mankind), is a pioneering work in the theory of maqasid ash-shari'ah (objectives of Islamic law). In it, he argued that all Islamic legal rulings ultimately serve the welfare of humanity, a framework that profoundly influenced later scholars such as ash-Shatibi and contemporary maqasid theorists.

Al-Izz is famous for confronting the Ayyubid rulers over their alliance with the Crusaders and for issuing a fatwa requiring that Mamluk emirs prove their freedom before holding positions of authority. His courageous stance earned him the title 'Sultan of the Scholars' and the saying that 'the scholars are many but the Izz is one.' He served as the chief judge of Egypt and shaped the legal and ethical landscape of the Ayyubid-Mamluk transition.

name_full_en: Abd al-Qadir ibn Muhammad Isa al-Halabi
title: Sheikh
bio_short_en: Syrian Shadhili scholar and author of 'Haqa'iq an at-Tasawwuf' (Realities of Sufism), a widely read defense and explanation of orthodox Sufism.
bio_en:
Abd al-Qadir ibn Muhammad Isa al-Halabi (1920-1991 CE / 1338-1412 AH) was a Syrian Islamic scholar and Shadhili Sufi master from Aleppo. He studied under leading scholars of Aleppo and Damascus and became a prominent figure in the city's scholarly and spiritual circles.

His best-known work, 'Haqa'iq an at-Tasawwuf' (Realities of Sufism), is one of the most widely read contemporary books on orthodox Sufism. The work systematically presents the principles, practices, and terminology of Sufism while grounding everything in the Quran, Sunnah, and the statements of recognized scholars. It has been translated into multiple languages and serves as a standard introduction to traditional Sufi thought.

Sheikh Abd al-Qadir was a Hanafi jurist and Ash'ari theologian who led a Shadhili-Darqawi circle in Aleppo. He emphasized the inseparability of outward Islamic law and inward spiritual practice, following the methodology of earlier Shadhili masters such as Ahmad Zarruq.

title: Sheikh
bio_short_en: Last Shaykh al-Islam of the Ottoman Caliphate and a fierce defender of the Hanafi-Maturidi tradition in the modern era.
bio_en:
Muhammad Zahid al-Kawthari (1879-1952 CE / 1296-1371 AH) was a Circassian-Turkish scholar who served as the adjunct to the last Shaykh al-Islam of the Ottoman Caliphate. Born in Duzce (in modern Turkey), he studied under Ottoman scholars and rose to prominence in the Ottoman religious establishment.

After the abolition of the Caliphate in 1924, al-Kawthari emigrated to Egypt, where he became a leading voice for traditional Hanafi-Maturidi scholarship. He was a vigorous polemicist who wrote extensively in defense of the Hanafi school and Ash'ari-Maturidi theology against what he perceived as literalist and modernist deviations.

He edited numerous classical manuscripts, wrote introductions to published works, and authored treatises on a wide range of Islamic sciences. His marginalia and introductions to classical texts are valued for their scholarly depth. Despite the controversy surrounding some of his polemical positions, his contribution to preserving and defending the Ottoman scholarly tradition in the modern period is widely acknowledged.

name_full_en: Abu al-Hasanat Muhammad Abd al-Hayy al-Lucknawi
title: Allama
bio_short_en: Indian Hanafi prodigy who authored over 100 scholarly works before dying at age 39, renowned for his encyclopedic knowledge across Islamic sciences.
bio_en:
Abu al-Hasanat Muhammad Abd al-Hayy al-Lucknawi (1848-1886 CE / 1264-1304 AH) was an Indian Hanafi scholar of extraordinary precocity and productivity. Despite living only 39 years, he authored over 100 works across jurisprudence, hadith sciences, biographical literature, and Arabic grammar.

His 'at-Ta'liq al-Mumajjad ala Muwatta Muhammad' is a celebrated commentary on Imam Muhammad ash-Shaybani's narration of the Muwatta. His 'ar-Raf' wat-Takmil fi al-Jarh wat-Ta'dil' is an important work on hadith narrator criticism. His biographical dictionary 'al-Fawa'id al-Bahiyyah fi Tarajim al-Hanafiyyah' catalogs the biographies of Hanafi scholars.

Al-Lucknawi was remarkable for his balanced scholarly approach. Though firmly Hanafi, he cited evidence from all schools and corrected what he saw as errors within the Hanafi tradition itself. His intellectual honesty and encyclopedic output at such a young age made him one of the most respected Hanafi scholars of the Indian subcontinent.

name_full_en: Muhammad Anwar Shah ibn Mu'azzam Shah al-Kashmiri
title: Allama
bio_short_en: Indian Deobandi scholar celebrated for his extraordinary memory and his hadith commentary 'Fayd al-Bari,' a masterwork on Sahih al-Bukhari.
bio_en:
Muhammad Anwar Shah ibn Mu'azzam Shah al-Kashmiri (1875-1933 CE / 1292-1352 AH) was one of the most brilliant Islamic scholars of the Indian subcontinent. Born in Kashmir, he studied at Darul Uloom Deoband and became its most celebrated teacher, serving as the head instructor of hadith.

His lectures on Sahih al-Bukhari, compiled by his student Badr-e Alam as 'Fayd al-Bari ala Sahih al-Bukhari,' are considered one of the most profound and comprehensive commentaries on the collection. He was known for his extraordinary memory, reportedly able to cite hadith narrations and their chains, along with narrator evaluations, entirely from memory.

Al-Kashmiri was a Hanafi jurist with deep engagement with the broader hadith tradition. His works demonstrate how Hanafi jurisprudence is supported by hadith evidence, countering the misconception that the Hanafi school neglects hadith. He authored numerous works in Arabic and was recognized as one of the greatest Muslim scholars of the 20th century.

name_full_en: Muhammad Hasyim Asy'ari ibn Abd al-Wahid
title: Sheikh
bio_short_en: Founder of Nahdlatul Ulama, the largest Islamic organization in the world, and a towering figure in Indonesian Islamic history.
bio_en:
Muhammad Hasyim Asy'ari (1871-1947 CE / 1287-1366 AH) was the founder of Nahdlatul Ulama (NU), the largest Islamic organization in the world with tens of millions of members, primarily in Indonesia. Born in Jombang, East Java, he studied under scholars in Java and at Masjid al-Haram in Mecca, including Ahmad Khatib al-Minangkabawi.

In 1926, he founded Nahdlatul Ulama to unite traditional Indonesian Muslim scholars (ulama) and preserve the Shafi'i madhhab, Ash'ari theology, and the Sufi tradition in the face of modernist and reformist challenges. NU became the primary vehicle for traditional Islam in Indonesia and played a crucial role in Indonesian independence.

Hasyim Asy'ari was a Shafi'i jurist and the founder of the Pesantren Tebuireng, one of the most important Islamic boarding schools in Java. His work 'Adab al-Alim wal-Muta'allim' (The Etiquette of the Teacher and the Student) is widely studied. He was posthumously declared a National Hero of Indonesia.

title: Sheikh
bio_short_en: Founder of Muhammadiyah, one of the largest Islamic organizations in Indonesia, and a pioneer of Islamic educational reform.
bio_en:
Muhammad Darwis, known as Ahmad Dahlan (1868-1923 CE / 1285-1341 AH), was the founder of Muhammadiyah, one of the largest Islamic organizations in Indonesia with tens of millions of members. Born in Yogyakarta, Java, he studied at Masjid al-Haram in Mecca under scholars including Ahmad Khatib al-Minangkabawi.

In 1912, he founded Muhammadiyah as an Islamic reform movement emphasizing return to the Quran and Sunnah, modern education, and social welfare. Under his leadership, Muhammadiyah established schools, hospitals, and orphanages that served communities regardless of religious affiliation. The organization became a major force for Islamic modernization in Indonesia.

Ahmad Dahlan was influenced by the reformist ideas of Muhammad Abduh and Rashid Rida while maintaining his own distinctive approach tailored to the Indonesian context. He was posthumously declared a National Hero of Indonesia.

name_full_en: Abu al-Qasim Hamzah ibn Yusuf al-Hamdhani al-Kinani as-Sahmi
title: Imam
bio_short_en: Historian and hadith scholar of Jurjan, author of 'Tarikh Jurjan,' a major biographical dictionary of scholars from the region.
bio_en:
Abu al-Qasim Hamzah ibn Yusuf al-Hamdhani al-Kinani as-Sahmi (935-1028 CE / 323-419 AH) was a hadith scholar and historian from Jurjan (modern Gorgan, Iran). He is best known for his work 'Tarikh Jurjan' (The History of Jurjan), a biographical dictionary documenting the scholars, hadith narrators, and notable figures from the Jurjan region.

This work is an invaluable source for the history of Islamic scholarship in the Caspian region and follows the tradition of local biographical dictionaries that preserve knowledge about scholars not covered in the major biographical collections. It contains entries on hundreds of scholars with information about their chains of narration, scholarly contributions, and biographical details.

As-Sahmi traveled extensively to collect hadith and study under scholars across the Muslim world. His work represents the rich scholarly tradition that existed in the smaller cities of the Islamic world, beyond the more famous centers of Baghdad, Damascus, and Cairo.

name_full_en: Abu Isma'il Abdullah ibn Muhammad al-Ansari al-Harawi
title: Sheikh
bio_short_en: Hanbali scholar of Herat and author of 'Manazil as-Sa'irin' (Stations of the Wayfarers), a classic Sufi manual on the spiritual path.
bio_en:
Abu Isma'il Abdullah ibn Muhammad al-Ansari al-Harawi (1006-1089 CE / 396-481 AH) was a Hanbali scholar, hadith master, and Sufi from Herat (in modern Afghanistan). He was one of the most prominent Hanbali scholars of Khurasan and is best known for his spiritual manual 'Manazil as-Sa'irin' (Stations of the Wayfarers).

'Manazil as-Sa'irin' presents a systematic framework of 100 spiritual stations organized in ten sections, each containing ten stations arranged in ascending order of spiritual progress. The work attracted the famous commentary by Ibn Qayyim al-Jawziyyah titled 'Madarij as-Salikin' (The Ranks of the Seekers), which remains one of the most studied books on Islamic spirituality.

Al-Harawi was a staunch defender of the Athari creed and faced persecution for his theological positions, being imprisoned and threatened with execution multiple times. Despite this, he remained firm in his beliefs. His combination of Hanbali legal and theological commitments with deep Sufi spirituality demonstrates that the early Hanbali tradition was not opposed to Sufism per se.

name_full_en: Majd ad-Din Abu Tahir Muhammad ibn Yaqub al-Fayruzabadi
title: Imam
bio_short_en: Author of 'al-Qamus al-Muhit,' one of the most famous and comprehensive dictionaries of the Arabic language.
bio_en:
Majd ad-Din Abu Tahir Muhammad ibn Yaqub al-Fayruzabadi (1329-1415 CE / 729-817 AH) was one of the greatest Arabic lexicographers in history. Born in Karazin near Shiraz, he traveled extensively across the Muslim world, studying and teaching in Baghdad, Damascus, Jerusalem, Cairo, Mecca, Delhi, and finally settling in Yemen.

His masterwork, 'al-Qamus al-Muhit' (The Encompassing Dictionary), is one of the most celebrated Arabic dictionaries ever compiled. It was a condensation of his even larger work 'al-Lami' al-Mu'allam al-Ujab' and became the standard reference dictionary of Arabic for centuries. The word 'qamus' itself became synonymous with 'dictionary' in Arabic and several other languages.

Al-Fayruzabadi served as the chief judge (qadi) of Yemen under the Rasulid dynasty. He was a Shafi'i jurist and hadith scholar in addition to his linguistic expertise. His dictionary was later expanded by az-Zabidi into the monumental 'Taj al-Arus.'

name_full_en: Jamal ad-Din Abu al-Fadl Muhammad ibn Mukarram ibn Manzur al-Ansari
bio_short_en: Author of 'Lisan al-Arab,' the most comprehensive classical dictionary of the Arabic language.
bio_en:
Jamal ad-Din Abu al-Fadl Muhammad ibn Mukarram ibn Manzur (1233-1312 CE / 630-711 AH) was an Egyptian lexicographer and author of 'Lisan al-Arab' (The Tongue of the Arabs), the most comprehensive dictionary of the Arabic language in the classical tradition.

'Lisan al-Arab' is a monumental work of approximately 80,000 entries that synthesizes and expands upon the major earlier Arabic dictionaries, including those of Ibn Sidah, al-Azhari, al-Jawhari, and Ibn Barri. It provides extensive citations from the Quran, hadith, pre-Islamic and Islamic poetry, and prose literature to illustrate the meanings and usages of words. The work remains the primary reference dictionary for classical Arabic.

Ibn Manzur was of North African (Libyan) origin but lived and worked in Egypt. He served in the Mamluk bureaucracy and was known for his prodigious literary output. In addition to 'Lisan al-Arab,' he produced abridgements of several major works of Arabic literature and history.

name_full_en: Abd ar-Ra'uf ibn Ali al-Jawi al-Fansuri as-Singkili
title: Sheikh
bio_short_en: Acehnese scholar who studied in Arabia and became the leading Islamic authority in Southeast Asia, producing the first complete Malay Quran commentary.
bio_en:
Abd ar-Ra'uf ibn Ali as-Singkili (1615-1693 CE / 1024-1105 AH) was a major Islamic scholar from Singkel, Aceh (in modern Indonesia) who played a pivotal role in shaping Islamic scholarship and practice in Southeast Asia. He studied for nineteen years in Arabia, including under the renowned hadith scholar Ibrahim al-Kurani in Medina.

Returning to Aceh, he served as the Mufti of the Acehnese Sultanate and became the leading authority on Islamic sciences in the region. His most significant work is 'Tarjuman al-Mustafid,' the first complete Quran commentary written in Malay, which drew primarily on the tafsirs of al-Baydawi and al-Jalalayn. The work served as the primary Quran commentary for Malay-speaking Muslims for centuries.

As-Singkili was a Shafi'i jurist and a Shattariyyah Sufi who promoted a balanced approach to Sufism grounded in Islamic law. He played an important role in resolving theological controversies in Aceh regarding pantheistic Sufi doctrines, advocating for orthodox Sufi positions that maintained the distinction between Creator and creation.

name_full_en: Burhan ad-Din Ibrahim ibn Muhammad al-Bajuri
title: Sheikh
bio_short_en: Grand Imam of al-Azhar and author of widely studied commentaries on Shafi'i fiqh and Ash'ari theology.
bio_en:
Burhan ad-Din Ibrahim ibn Muhammad al-Bajuri (1784-1860 CE / 1198-1276 AH) was the Grand Imam of al-Azhar from 1847 until his death, and one of the most influential Egyptian scholars of the 19th century. Born in Bajur, Monufia Governorate, he studied at al-Azhar and rose to become its head.

His hashiyah (super-commentary) on Ibn Qasim al-Ghazzi's 'Fath al-Qarib' (a commentary on Abu Shuja's Shafi'i fiqh manual) became one of the most widely studied texts in Shafi'i legal education. His 'Tuhfat al-Murid ala Jawharat at-Tawhid' (a commentary on Ibrahim al-Laqqani's creed poem) is similarly foundational in the study of Ash'ari theology across the Muslim world.

Al-Bajuri led al-Azhar during a period of significant change in Egypt and worked to maintain the institution's scholarly standards. His works are characterized by clarity, thoroughness, and accessibility, which contributed to their widespread adoption in traditional Islamic curricula from Egypt to Southeast Asia.

name_full_en: Ahmad ibn Zayn ibn Ahmad Dahlan
title: Sheikh
bio_short_en: Mufti of the Shafi'i school in Mecca and historian, author of works on Prophetic biography and the history of Mecca.
bio_en:
Ahmad ibn Zayn ibn Ahmad Dahlan (1816-1886 CE / 1231-1304 AH) was the Mufti of the Shafi'i school at Masjid al-Haram in Mecca and a leading scholar of the Hijaz. Born in Mecca, he studied under the scholars of the Sacred Mosque and became one of the foremost religious authorities of his time.

He authored several important works, including 'as-Sirah an-Nabawiyyah' (The Prophetic Biography), a popular and accessible seerah text, and 'Tarikh ad-Duwal al-Islamiyyah' (History of Islamic States), a historical overview of Muslim dynasties. His 'Khulasat al-Kalam fi Bayan Umara al-Balad al-Haram' is a history of the rulers of Mecca.

Dahlan was a Shafi'i jurist and Ash'ari theologian who also wrote on theological topics. His works have been widely studied in the Hijaz, Southeast Asia, and other regions where the Shafi'i school predominates.

name_full_en: Shihab ad-Din Ahmad ibn Hamzah ar-Ramli
title: Imam
bio_short_en: Egyptian Shafi'i jurist and father of Shams ad-Din ar-Ramli, both major authorities in later Shafi'i jurisprudence.
bio_en:
Shihab ad-Din Ahmad ibn Hamzah ar-Ramli (1472-1550 CE / 877-957 AH) was a leading Shafi'i jurist of Mamluk and early Ottoman Egypt. Born in Ramla, Palestine, he settled in Cairo where he became one of the foremost Shafi'i authorities of his era.

He authored 'Sharh Safwat az-Zubad,' a commentary on a versified summary of Shafi'i fiqh, and other legal works. He was the father and teacher of Shams ad-Din Muhammad ar-Ramli (known as 'ash-Shafi'i as-Saghir,' the Little Shafi'i), who would become one of the most authoritative voices in later Shafi'i jurisprudence. Together, father and son represent a golden era of Shafi'i legal scholarship in Egypt.

Shihab ad-Din ar-Ramli was known for his piety, his deep knowledge of Shafi'i law, and his role in training the next generation of jurists who would shape the Shafi'i school during the Ottoman period.

name_full_en: Abu Abdullah Muhammad ibn Muhammad ibn al-Hajj al-Abdari al-Fasi
bio_short_en: Maliki jurist and author of 'al-Madkhal,' a detailed work on Islamic etiquette, religious practices, and social customs.
bio_en:
Abu Abdullah Muhammad ibn Muhammad ibn al-Hajj al-Abdari (1258-1336 CE / 656-737 AH) was a Maliki jurist from Fez, Morocco, who settled in Cairo. He is best known for his major work 'al-Madkhal' (The Introduction), a comprehensive guide to proper Islamic practice and etiquette.

'Al-Madkhal' covers a vast range of topics including the proper conduct of worship, the etiquette of seeking knowledge, social customs, marriage practices, commercial dealings, and daily life. The work is notable for its detailed critique of innovations (bida') and customs that Ibn al-Hajj considered contrary to the Sunnah. It provides invaluable insight into the social and religious life of Muslims in Mamluk-era Cairo.

Ibn al-Hajj followed the Maliki school and was deeply influenced by the teachings of Imam Malik on the importance of following the practice of the people of Medina. His work reflects a concern for preserving the authentic practices of Islam and correcting widespread deviations.

name_full_en: Abu al-Fadl Majd ad-Din Abdullah ibn Mahmud al-Mawsili
title: Imam
bio_short_en: Hanafi jurist from Mosul, author of 'al-Ikhtiyar li Ta'lil al-Mukhtar,' a widely studied manual of Hanafi fiqh.
bio_en:
Abu al-Fadl Majd ad-Din Abdullah ibn Mahmud al-Mawsili (1203-1284 CE / 599-683 AH) was a prominent Hanafi jurist from Mosul, Iraq. He authored several important works in Hanafi jurisprudence that became standard texts in the Hanafi legal curriculum.

His most famous work is 'al-Ikhtiyar li Ta'lil al-Mukhtar' (The Selection for the Explanation of the Chosen), which is his own commentary on his earlier text 'al-Mukhtar lil-Fatwa' (The Chosen for Legal Opinions). The work presents Hanafi legal rulings with their supporting evidence and reasoning, making it a comprehensive and accessible reference for Hanafi fiqh. It has been widely studied in Hanafi educational institutions, particularly in the Indian subcontinent and the Arab world.

Al-Mawsili was active during the Mongol invasions and the turbulent period of the 13th century. Despite the political upheaval, he contributed significantly to the preservation and transmission of the Hanafi legal tradition.

name_full_en: Zayn ad-Din ibn Ibrahim ibn Muhammad ibn Nujaym al-Misri
title: Imam
bio_short_en: Egyptian Hanafi jurist, author of 'al-Ashbah wan-Naza'ir,' a masterwork on Islamic legal maxims and principles.
bio_en:
Zayn ad-Din ibn Ibrahim ibn Nujaym al-Misri (1520-1563 CE / 926-970 AH) was an Egyptian Hanafi jurist who produced some of the most important works in Hanafi legal theory despite his short life. Born in Cairo, he studied under the scholars of al-Azhar and the Hanafi legal institutions of Ottoman Egypt.

His most influential work, 'al-Ashbah wan-Naza'ir' (The Similarities and Parallels), is a systematic presentation of Islamic legal maxims (qawa'id fiqhiyyah) from the Hanafi perspective. The work demonstrates how a relatively small number of overarching legal principles can generate rulings for an enormous range of practical cases. It became a foundational text in the study of Islamic legal theory.

He also authored 'al-Bahr ar-Ra'iq Sharh Kanz ad-Daqa'iq' (The Limpid Sea: Commentary on the Treasury of Subtleties), a detailed commentary on an-Nasafi's Hanafi fiqh manual. His works are characterized by sharp legal reasoning and remain essential references in Hanafi jurisprudence.

name_full_en: Muhammad ibn Isma'il ibn Salah al-Amir as-San'ani
title: Imam
bio_short_en: Yemeni scholar and author of 'Subul as-Salam,' the famous commentary on Ibn Hajar's 'Bulugh al-Maram' on hadiths of legal rulings.
bio_en:
Muhammad ibn Isma'il al-Amir as-San'ani (1687-1768 CE / 1099-1182 AH) was a Yemeni scholar of renown who called for independent legal reasoning (ijtihad) based on the Quran and Sunnah. Born in Kahlan, Yemen, and raised in San'a, he studied under the scholars of Yemen and became one of its leading intellectual figures.

His most famous work, 'Subul as-Salam Sharh Bulugh al-Maram' (The Paths of Peace: Commentary on the Attainment of the Objective), is a commentary on Ibn Hajar al-Asqalani's collection of hadiths related to legal rulings. The work extracts legal principles from each hadith and presents the positions of the various schools, becoming one of the most widely studied hadith-fiqh texts globally.

As-San'ani was an advocate of ijtihad (independent legal reasoning) and opposed strict adherence to a single madhab without evidence. Though born into a Zaydi family, he moved toward Sunni positions on many issues, drawing on the hadith-based approach that characterized Yemeni scholarship.

name_full_en: Zayn ad-Din Muhammad Abd ar-Ra'uf al-Munawi
title: Imam
bio_short_en: Egyptian scholar and author of 'Fayd al-Qadir,' the most extensive commentary on as-Suyuti's 'al-Jami as-Saghir' hadith collection.
bio_en:
Zayn ad-Din Muhammad Abd ar-Ra'uf al-Munawi (1545-1621 CE / 952-1031 AH) was an Egyptian Shafi'i scholar and one of the most prolific hadith commentators of the Ottoman period. Born in Cairo, he studied under the leading scholars of his time and became a major scholarly figure at al-Azhar.

His magnum opus, 'Fayd al-Qadir Sharh al-Jami as-Saghir' (The Overflowing Grace: Commentary on the Small Collection), is the most extensive and detailed commentary on Imam as-Suyuti's popular hadith collection 'al-Jami as-Saghir.' For each hadith, al-Munawi provides the full chain of narration, evaluates its authenticity, explains its meaning, and draws out legal and spiritual lessons. The work became an indispensable reference for scholars and students of hadith.

Al-Munawi also authored 'at-Taysir bi Sharh al-Jami as-Saghir' (a shorter commentary on the same collection) and 'al-Kawakib ad-Durriyyah fi Tarajim as-Sadah as-Sufiyyah,' biographical entries on Sufi figures. He was a Shafi'i jurist with deep knowledge of hadith sciences and Sufi spirituality.

name_full_en: Isma'il Haqqi ibn Mustafa al-Istanbuli al-Bursawi
title: Sheikh
bio_short_en: Ottoman Sufi scholar and mufassir, author of 'Ruh al-Bayan,' a comprehensive Quran commentary blending exoteric and esoteric interpretation.
bio_en:
Isma'il Haqqi ibn Mustafa al-Bursawi (1653-1725 CE / 1063-1137 AH) was an Ottoman Sufi scholar, Quran commentator, and Jelveti (Khalwati) Sufi master. Born in Istanbul, he eventually settled in Bursa, where he taught and wrote for much of his life.

His masterwork, 'Ruh al-Bayan fi Tafsir al-Quran' (The Spirit of Elucidation in Quran Commentary), is one of the most comprehensive Ottoman-era tafsirs. It combines legal, theological, and linguistic exegesis with Sufi spiritual interpretations, presenting a rich multi-layered reading of the Quran. The work draws on dozens of earlier commentaries and adds original insights, particularly from the Sufi perspective.

Al-Bursawi authored works in both Arabic and Turkish on Sufism, ethics, and Quranic sciences. He was a prominent figure in the intellectual and spiritual life of Ottoman Bursa and his tafsir continues to be studied by scholars interested in the Sufi approach to Quranic interpretation.

name_full_en: Abu Sa'id Muhammad ibn Muhammad al-Khadimi
bio_short_en: Ottoman Hanafi scholar, author of 'Bariqa Mahmudiyyah,' a commentary on 'at-Tariqa al-Muhammadiyyah' on Islamic ethics and practice.
bio_en:
Abu Sa'id Muhammad ibn Muhammad al-Khadimi (1701-1762 CE / 1113-1176 AH) was an Ottoman Hanafi scholar from Khadim, near Konya, in central Anatolia. He became one of the most widely read authors in the Ottoman scholarly tradition.

His most famous work, 'al-Bariqa al-Mahmudiyyah fi Sharh at-Tariqa al-Muhammadiyyah' (The Mahmudian Lightning: Commentary on the Muhammadan Way), is a detailed commentary on al-Birjawi's ethical treatise. The work covers Islamic ethics, proper conduct, and the inner and outer dimensions of worship, drawing on Hanafi jurisprudence and Sufi insights. It became a standard text in Ottoman religious education.

Al-Khadimi also authored works on Hanafi fiqh, usul al-fiqh, and Islamic legal maxims. His writings are characterized by their practical orientation and their synthesis of legal and spiritual concerns. He represents the vibrant scholarly tradition of Anatolian Ottoman scholars.

name_full_en: Shams ad-Din Muhammad ibn Muhammad ibn Amir Hajj al-Halabi
bio_short_en: Hanafi scholar from Aleppo, author of 'at-Taqrir wat-Tahbir,' an important commentary on Hanafi usul al-fiqh.
bio_en:
Shams ad-Din Muhammad ibn Muhammad ibn Amir Hajj al-Halabi (c. 1420-1474 CE / 823-879 AH) was a Hanafi jurist from Aleppo, Syria. He is known for his works on Hanafi legal methodology and principles of Islamic jurisprudence.

His most important work is 'at-Taqrir wat-Tahbir' (The Report and Refinement), a commentary on Ibn al-Humam's 'at-Tahrir fi Usul al-Fiqh,' one of the key texts on Hanafi legal theory. He also authored 'Hal al-Rumuz,' a legal commentary. His works demonstrate the ongoing refinement and elaboration of Hanafi legal methodology during the late Mamluk period.

Ibn Amir Hajj lived during the transition from Mamluk to early Ottoman rule and contributed to maintaining the intellectual vitality of the Hanafi scholarly tradition in Syria.

name_full_en: Ala ad-Din Muhammad ibn Muhammad Amin ibn Abidin
bio_short_en: Son of the great Hanafi jurist Ibn Abidin, who continued his father's legacy with supplements and additions to the 'Radd al-Muhtar.'
bio_en:
Ala ad-Din Muhammad ibn Muhammad Amin ibn Abidin (1827-1889 CE / 1244-1306 AH) was a Damascene Hanafi jurist and the son of the great Ibn Abidin, author of the celebrated 'Radd al-Muhtar ala ad-Durr al-Mukhtar.' He continued and supplemented his father's monumental work.

His most notable contribution is 'Qurrat Uyun al-Akhyar Takmilat Radd al-Muhtar' (The Delight of the Eyes of the Righteous: Supplement to the Radd al-Muhtar), which completes and updates his father's work on areas that were left unfinished or that required additional elaboration. He also authored independent works on Hanafi jurisprudence.

Ala ad-Din ibn Abidin maintained the scholarly standards set by his father and helped ensure that the 'Radd al-Muhtar' became the definitive reference in the Hanafi school. The father-son scholarly legacy represents one of the most important contributions to late Hanafi jurisprudence.

name_full_en: Ibrahim ibn Ibrahim ibn Hasan al-Laqqani al-Maliki
title: Sheikh
bio_short_en: Egyptian Maliki scholar and author of 'Jawharat at-Tawhid,' a famous creed poem on Ash'ari theology that is widely memorized.
bio_en:
Ibrahim ibn Ibrahim al-Laqqani (1567-1632 CE / 975-1041 AH) was an Egyptian Maliki scholar and theologian who taught at al-Azhar. He is best known for his didactic poem 'Jawharat at-Tawhid' (The Jewel of Monotheism), a versified presentation of Ash'ari theology.

The 'Jawharah' became one of the most widely memorized and studied creed texts in the Muslim world, particularly in Maliki scholarly circles. The poem covers the essential theological beliefs of Sunni Islam in a concise, elegant format. It attracted numerous commentaries, the most famous being al-Bajuri's 'Tuhfat al-Murid,' which is itself a standard theology textbook.

Al-Laqqani was also a hadith scholar and faqih. He came from the town of Laqqanah in Egypt and spent his career teaching at al-Azhar. His poem's success lies in its combination of doctrinal precision with poetic accessibility, making complex theological concepts memorable for students.

title: Sheikh
bio_short_en: West African Fulani scholar who settled in Medina and authored 'Iqa dh al-Himam,' a work on following evidence over blind imitation.
bio_en:
Salih ibn Muhammad al-Fulani (1752-1803 CE / 1166-1218 AH) was a West African Islamic scholar of Fulani origin who became a leading intellectual figure in Medina. Born in the Futa Toro region (modern Senegal), he traveled eastward in pursuit of knowledge, eventually settling in Medina where he spent the rest of his life teaching and writing.

His most famous work, 'Iqadh al-Himam fi al-Amal bil-Hadith was-Sunan' (Awakening Resolution in Acting upon Hadith and the Sunan), is a significant treatise arguing for the importance of following authenticated hadith evidence rather than relying solely on established madhab positions when the evidence is clear. The work cites numerous scholars from all four madhabs who supported this principle.

Al-Fulani represents the vibrant tradition of West African Islamic scholarship and the connections between sub-Saharan African Muslims and the scholarly centers of the Hijaz. His presence in Medina as a respected scholar demonstrates the universal nature of Islamic learning.

name_full_en: Muhyi ad-Din Abu Abdullah Muhammad ibn Ali ibn Arabi al-Hatimi at-Ta'i
title: Sheikh
bio_short_en: Known as 'ash-Shaykh al-Akbar' (the Greatest Master), one of the most influential and controversial figures in Islamic mysticism.
bio_en:
Muhyi ad-Din Abu Abdullah Muhammad ibn Ali ibn Arabi (1165-1240 CE / 560-638 AH), known as ash-Shaykh al-Akbar (the Greatest Master) and Ibn Arabi, was one of the most influential and controversial figures in the history of Islamic thought. Born in Murcia, al-Andalus, he spent his youth in Seville before traveling extensively throughout the Muslim world, eventually settling in Damascus.

His magnum opus, 'al-Futuhat al-Makkiyyah' (The Meccan Revelations), is a massive encyclopedic work of Sufi metaphysics, cosmology, and spiritual knowledge that he began writing during a pilgrimage to Mecca. His other major work, 'Fusus al-Hikam' (The Bezels of Wisdom), presents the spiritual wisdom associated with each of the major prophets. These works developed a complex metaphysical system that profoundly influenced subsequent Islamic thought.

Ibn Arabi's concept of 'wahdat al-wujud' (the unity of existence), as understood by his interpreters, became one of the most debated ideas in Islamic intellectual history. Scholars have been divided about his teachings: supporters see him as the greatest Sufi master who articulated the deepest truths of Islamic spirituality, while critics argue some of his formulations conflict with orthodox theology. This debate continues among Muslim scholars today. He is buried in Damascus, where his tomb remains a site of visitation.

name_full_en: Abu al-Hasan Ali ibn Abd ar-Rahman al-Fasi
bio_short_en: Moroccan Maliki scholar of Fez and author of a commentary on the Mukhtasar of Khalil, an important reference in Maliki fiqh.
bio_en:
Abu al-Hasan Ali ibn Abd ar-Rahman al-Fasi (1630-1685 CE / 1040-1096 AH) was a Moroccan Maliki scholar from Fez who contributed significantly to Maliki legal scholarship during the early Alawid period. He came from the prominent al-Fasi scholarly family.

He authored works on Maliki jurisprudence, including commentary on the Mukhtasar of Khalil, and was involved in the intellectual life of Fez. He served as a judge and teacher, training students who would go on to become important scholars in their own right.

Al-Fasi represents the continuous scholarly tradition of Fez, Morocco, which served as one of the great centers of Maliki learning for centuries. His family produced multiple generations of scholars who contributed to the legal, theological, and literary heritage of the region.

name_full_en: Abu Abdullah Muhammad ibn Abdullah al-Kharashi
title: Sheikh
bio_short_en: First Maliki Grand Imam of al-Azhar, author of the most widely studied commentary on Khalil's Mukhtasar.
bio_en:
Abu Abdullah Muhammad ibn Abdullah al-Kharashi (c. 1610-1690 CE / 1019-1101 AH) was an Egyptian Maliki jurist who became the first Grand Imam of al-Azhar to be formally appointed from the Maliki school. He served as Sheikh al-Azhar and was one of the most prominent scholars of his era.

His most important work is his commentary on Khalil ibn Ishaq's 'Mukhtasar,' which became the most widely studied and accessible explanation of this foundational Maliki legal text. The commentary is valued for its clarity and its ability to make the extremely concise language of Khalil's Mukhtasar understandable to students.

Al-Kharashi's position as Grand Imam of al-Azhar and the widespread adoption of his commentary cemented his influence on Maliki legal education in Egypt and beyond. His work continues to be studied in traditional Maliki curricula.

name_full_en: Abu Isa al-Mahdi ibn Muhammad al-Wazzani
bio_short_en: Moroccan Maliki mufti and author of 'al-Mi'yar al-Jadid,' a massive collection of Maliki fatwas from North Africa.
bio_en:
Abu Isa al-Mahdi ibn Muhammad al-Wazzani (1849-1923 CE / 1266-1342 AH) was a Moroccan Maliki jurist and mufti who produced one of the most extensive collections of Maliki legal opinions in North African history. He served as a leading mufti in Fez and was consulted on legal matters from across Morocco.

His monumental work 'al-Mi'yar al-Jadid al-Jami al-Mu'rib an Fatawa Ahl Ifriqiyya wal-Andalus wal-Maghrib' (The New Standard: A Comprehensive Collection of Fatwas from the People of Ifriqiya, Andalusia, and the Maghreb) continues the tradition of al-Wansharisi's earlier 'al-Mi'yar.' It gathers and organizes thousands of legal opinions from North African and Andalusian Maliki scholars, providing an invaluable resource for understanding the application of Maliki law in diverse contexts.

Al-Wazzani also authored 'an-Nawazil al-Jadidah al-Kubra' (The Great New Legal Cases), addressing contemporary legal questions. His works represent the culmination of the Moroccan tradition of Maliki legal compilation.

name_full_en: Abu al-Fadl Iyad ibn Musa ibn Iyad al-Yahsubi as-Sabti
title: Qadi
bio_short_en: Maliki judge of Ceuta and author of 'ash-Shifa bi Ta'rif Huquq al-Mustafa,' the most celebrated work on the rights and honor of the Prophet Muhammad.
bio_en:
Abu al-Fadl Iyad ibn Musa al-Yahsubi (1083-1149 CE / 476-544 AH), commonly known as al-Qadi Iyad, was a Maliki jurist, hadith scholar, and judge from Ceuta (in modern Morocco). He is one of the most revered scholars in the Maliki tradition and in the broader Muslim world.

His most famous work, 'ash-Shifa bi Ta'rif Huquq al-Mustafa' (The Cure through the Definition of the Rights of the Chosen One), is the most celebrated book on the status, characteristics, and rights of the Prophet Muhammad in Islamic literature. The work covers the obligation of loving and respecting the Prophet, his physical and moral qualities, his miracles, and the legal rulings related to those who insult him. It has been read, taught, and commented upon across the Muslim world for nearly a millennium.

Al-Qadi Iyad also authored 'Ikmal al-Mu'lim bi Fawa'id Muslim' (Completion of the Teacher with the Benefits of [Sahih] Muslim), an important commentary on Sahih Muslim, and 'Tartib al-Madarik,' a biographical dictionary of Maliki scholars. He served as the qadi of Ceuta and later Granada. He is buried in Marrakesh, where his tomb is still visited.

name_full_en: Abu Ishaq Ibrahim ibn Ali ibn Yusuf ash-Shirazi
title: Imam
bio_short_en: Shafi'i jurist and first teacher at the Nizamiyyah Madrasa in Baghdad, author of 'al-Muhadhdhab' and 'at-Tanbih' in Shafi'i fiqh.
bio_en:
Abu Ishaq Ibrahim ibn Ali ash-Shirazi (1003-1083 CE / 393-476 AH) was one of the most important Shafi'i jurists of the classical period. Born in Firuzabad, Fars (Iran), he traveled to Baghdad where he became the leading Shafi'i scholar of the city and was appointed as the first teacher at the famous Nizamiyyah Madrasa.

His 'al-Muhadhdhab fi Fiqh al-Imam ash-Shafi'i' (The Refined Work in Shafi'i Jurisprudence) became one of the two main references in the Shafi'i school (alongside al-Rafi'i's work). Imam an-Nawawi later wrote his monumental 'al-Majmu' as a commentary on al-Muhadhdhab. His shorter work 'at-Tanbih' (The Alert) served as a widely used introductory text in Shafi'i fiqh.

He also authored 'al-Luma' fi Usul al-Fiqh' (Gleams in Legal Theory), one of the foundational texts on Shafi'i legal methodology. Abu Ishaq was known for his humility, piety, and skill in debate. His students included many scholars who would continue the Shafi'i tradition in Baghdad.

name_full_en: Abu al-Qasim Abd al-Karim ibn Muhammad ar-Rafi'i al-Qazwini
title: Imam
bio_short_en: Major Shafi'i jurist from Qazwin whose 'al-Aziz' served as one of the two foundational texts that an-Nawawi later refined in his works.
bio_en:
Abu al-Qasim Abd al-Karim ibn Muhammad ar-Rafi'i al-Qazwini (1160-1226 CE / 555-623 AH) was a major Shafi'i jurist from Qazwin (in modern Iran). Along with an-Nawawi, he is considered one of the two pillars of later Shafi'i jurisprudence.

His main work, 'Fath al-Aziz fi Sharh al-Wajiz' (commonly known as 'ash-Sharh al-Kabir' or 'al-Aziz'), is a comprehensive commentary on al-Ghazali's 'al-Wajiz' in Shafi'i fiqh. This massive work examined legal issues in great detail and became one of the two main references that an-Nawawi used when composing his 'Rawdat at-Talibin' and 'al-Minhaj.' The methodological tradition of combining ar-Rafi'i and an-Nawawi's tarjih (preference) became the standard for later Shafi'i jurisprudence, with their combined agreement being termed the 'mu'tamad' (relied-upon) position of the school.

Ar-Rafi'i was also a hadith scholar and historian. His biographical dictionary 'at-Tadwin fi Akhbar Qazwin' documents the scholars and history of his hometown. He is a crucial figure in understanding the development of the Shafi'i school into its mature form.

name_full_en: Shihab ad-Din Ahmad ibn al-Husayn Abu Shuja' al-Isfahani
bio_short_en: Shafi'i jurist from Isfahan, author of 'al-Ghayah wat-Taqrib,' the most widely studied introductory Shafi'i fiqh manual.
bio_en:
Shihab ad-Din Ahmad ibn al-Husayn Abu Shuja' al-Isfahani (1042-1106 CE / 434-500 AH) was a Shafi'i jurist from Isfahan known for authoring one of the most beloved and widely taught texts in Islamic legal education.

His 'al-Ghayah wat-Taqrib' (The Aim and the Approximation), also known as 'Matn Abi Shuja' or 'Mukhtasar Abi Shuja,' is a concise primer on Shafi'i jurisprudence covering purification, prayer, zakat, fasting, hajj, commercial transactions, inheritance, criminal law, and other legal topics. Despite its brevity, it systematically covers the essential rulings of the Shafi'i school.

The text's clarity and conciseness made it the standard introductory fiqh manual for beginners in the Shafi'i tradition, studied from Egypt and Yemen to Southeast Asia. It attracted numerous commentaries, the most famous being 'Fath al-Qarib' by Ibn Qasim al-Ghazzi and 'al-Iqna' by al-Khatib ash-Shirbini. Abu Shuja' reportedly wrote the text while performing devotional retreat in Medina.

name_full_en: Abdullah ibn Alawi ibn Muhammad al-Haddad
title: Imam
bio_short_en: Yemeni Ba'Alawi scholar and Sufi master from Tarim, author of influential spiritual works including 'Risalat al-Mu'awanah' and 'an-Nasa'ih ad-Diniyyah.'
bio_en:
Abdullah ibn Alawi ibn Muhammad al-Haddad (1634-1720 CE / 1044-1132 AH) was a Ba'Alawi scholar, Sufi master, and one of the great spiritual figures of Hadhramaut, Yemen. Born in Tarim into the distinguished Alawide sayyid lineage, he lost his eyesight in childhood but went on to become one of the most influential scholars in the Ba'Alawi Sufi tradition.

His writings focus on the practical dimensions of spiritual life and are characterized by their simplicity, sincerity, and accessibility. His most widely read works include 'Risalat al-Mu'awanah' (The Book of Assistance), a guide to spiritual practice for everyday Muslims; 'an-Nasa'ih ad-Diniyyah' (Sincere Counsel), exhortations to righteousness; and 'ad-Da'wah at-Tammah' (The Complete Invitation), a comprehensive call to Islamic practice.

Imam al-Haddad composed numerous devotional litanies (awrad and ratib) that are recited by millions of Muslims worldwide, particularly in Yemen, East Africa, and Southeast Asia. His 'Ratib al-Haddad' is recited congregationally in mosques across the Islamic world. He is regarded as the 'renewer' (mujaddid) of the 12th Islamic century in the Ba'Alawi tradition.

name_full_en: Abu Abd ar-Rahman Habib ibn Umar ibn Ahmad Mashur al-Ba'Alawi
bio_short_en: Hadhrami Ba'Alawi scholar who authored 'Simthut Thahabin,' a devotional poem for the Prophet Muhammad widely chanted in Southeast Asia.
bio_en:
Habib Ali ibn Muhammad al-Habshi (1843-1915 CE / 1259-1333 AH) was a Hadhrami Ba'Alawi scholar who settled in the Indonesian archipelago and became one of the most beloved religious figures among Southeast Asian Muslims. He is best known for composing 'Simthut Thahabin' (The Necklace of Gold), a praise poem (mawlid) for the Prophet Muhammad.

'Simthut Thahabin' is one of the most widely recited mawlid texts in Southeast Asia, read at gatherings commemorating the Prophet's birth and other religious occasions. The work expresses deep love for the Prophet through elegant Arabic poetry.

Al-Habshi represents the influential community of Hadhrami scholars who settled across the Indian Ocean world, from East Africa to the Malay archipelago, spreading the Ba'Alawi scholarly and spiritual tradition. He was a Shafi'i jurist and a practitioner of the Ba'Alawi Sufi path.

name_full_en: Abu al-Wafa Ali ibn Aqil ibn Muhammad al-Baghdadi
title: Imam
bio_short_en: Hanbali jurist and theologian of Baghdad, author of 'al-Funun,' reportedly the largest book ever written in Arabic.
bio_en:
Abu al-Wafa Ali ibn Aqil al-Baghdadi (1040-1119 CE / 431-513 AH) was one of the most brilliant and original Hanbali scholars of Baghdad. He studied under leading Hanbali scholars and became the head of the Hanbali school in the city during a period of intense intellectual activity.

His most remarkable work, 'al-Funun' (The Arts), is reportedly the largest book ever written in the Arabic language. Estimates of its original size range from 400 to 800 volumes, though only fragments survive. It was an encyclopedic work covering every aspect of Islamic and general knowledge, written as a kind of intellectual diary over many years.

Ibn Aqil was known for his independent thinking and occasionally took positions that brought him into conflict with more conservative members of his school. He studied briefly with Mu'tazili teachers, which caused controversy, but he publicly retracted any heterodox positions. His 'al-Wadih fi Usul al-Fiqh' is an important work on Hanbali legal methodology. Ibn al-Jawzi praised him as the greatest mind the Hanbali school had produced.

name_full_en: Muhammad ibn al-Husayn Abu Ya'la al-Farra' al-Baghdadi
title: Qadi
bio_short_en: Chief Hanbali judge of Baghdad and systematizer of Hanbali theology, author of 'al-Ahkam as-Sultaniyyah' and 'al-Mu'tamad fi Usul ad-Din.'
bio_en:
Muhammad ibn al-Husayn Abu Ya'la al-Farra' (990-1066 CE / 380-458 AH) was the chief Hanbali judge of Baghdad and one of the most important systematizers of Hanbali jurisprudence and theology. He held the position of qadi for the Hanbali community in Baghdad during a period when the school was consolidating its legal and theological identity.

His 'al-Ahkam as-Sultaniyyah' (The Rules of Governance) is a work on Islamic political theory from the Hanbali perspective, paralleling al-Mawardi's work of the same title from the Shafi'i school. His 'al-Mu'tamad fi Usul ad-Din' (The Relied-Upon in the Principles of Religion) is an important text on Hanbali theology.

Abu Ya'la played a crucial role in developing the institutional framework of the Hanbali school in Baghdad, training a generation of scholars who would carry the tradition forward. His students included Ibn Aqil and other major Hanbali figures. His works on governance and theology contributed to the Hanbali school's distinctive approach to both fields.

name_full_en: Shams ad-Din Abu Abdullah Muhammad ibn Muflih al-Maqdisi
title: Imam
bio_short_en: Leading student of Ibn Taymiyyah and author of 'al-Furu' and 'al-Adab ash-Shar'iyyah,' major references in Hanbali fiqh and Islamic ethics.
bio_en:
