Shams ad-Din Abu Abdullah Muhammad ibn Muflih al-Maqdisi (1312-1362 CE / 712-763 AH) was one of the foremost Hanbali jurists of the 14th century and one of the most distinguished students of Ibn Taymiyyah. He settled in Damascus and became a leading authority on Hanbali jurisprudence.

His magnum opus, 'al-Furu'' (The Branches), is one of the most comprehensive works on Hanbali fiqh, presenting the school's positions with careful attention to the various opinions within the madhab, including the choices of Ibn Taymiyyah. It became a primary reference for Hanbali scholars. Ibn Taymiyyah reportedly said of him: 'You are not Ibn Muflih, you are Muflih itself' (a play on his name, which means 'successful').

His other major work, 'al-Adab ash-Shar'iyyah' (Islamic Etiquette), is a comprehensive guide to Islamic manners covering every aspect of daily life, from eating and drinking to travel, commerce, and social interactions. It remains one of the most widely referenced works on Islamic adab in the Hanbali tradition.

name_full_en: Mansur ibn Yunus ibn Salah ad-Din al-Buhuti
title: Sheikh
bio_short_en: Egyptian Hanbali jurist and author of 'Kashshaf al-Qina' and 'Sharh Muntaha al-Iradat,' the two definitive references of late Hanbali jurisprudence.
bio_en:
Mansur ibn Yunus al-Buhuti (1591-1641 CE / 1000-1051 AH) was an Egyptian Hanbali jurist whose works became the definitive references in the Hanbali school. Born in Buhut, Egypt, he studied in Cairo and became the leading Hanbali authority of his era.

His two most important works are 'Kashshaf al-Qina' an Matn al-Iqna'' (Removing the Veil from the Text of al-Iqna'), a comprehensive commentary on al-Hajjawi's 'al-Iqna',' and 'Sharh Muntaha al-Iradat' (Commentary on the Ultimate Goal of Intentions), a commentary on Ibn an-Najjar al-Futuhi's work. These two commentaries became the standard references for legal rulings in the Hanbali school, and the tradition of relying on them continues, particularly in Saudi Arabian courts.

Al-Buhuti also wrote 'ar-Rawd al-Murbi' Sharh Zad al-Mustaqni'' (The Refreshing Garden: Commentary on the Provision of the Independent), a popular intermediate-level Hanbali text. His works are characterized by their clarity and careful presentation of the school's established positions.

name_full_en: Abu al-Hasan Muhammad Hayat ibn Ibrahim as-Sindhi
title: Sheikh
bio_short_en: Sindhi hadith scholar based in Medina who taught Shah Waliullah Dehlawi and Muhammad ibn Abdul-Wahhab, influencing major reform movements.
bio_en:
Abu al-Hasan Muhammad Hayat ibn Ibrahim as-Sindhi (c. 1690-1750 CE / 1102-1163 AH) was a hadith scholar originally from Sindh (in modern Pakistan) who settled in Medina and became one of the most influential teachers of the 18th century.

His significance lies partly in the remarkable students he taught: among them were Shah Waliullah Dehlawi, who would transform Islamic scholarship in the Indian subcontinent, and Muhammad ibn Abdul-Wahhab, who would found the Wahhabi movement in Arabia. Through these students, as-Sindhi's emphasis on returning to the Quran and authentic Sunnah influenced major Islamic reform movements across the Muslim world.

As-Sindhi was a continuation of the Medinan scholarly tradition and taught hadith, placing emphasis on independent engagement with the hadith texts rather than mere adherence to a single school of law. His role in shaping two of the most significant reform movements of the 18th century, despite their very different trajectories, underscores his pivotal position in modern Islamic intellectual history.

name_full_en: Abu al-Hasan Ali ibn Abd al-Hayy al-Hasani an-Nadwi
title: Sheikh
bio_short_en: Indian Islamic scholar and author of 'Madha Khasira al-Alam bi Inhitat al-Muslimin' (What the World Lost by the Decline of Muslims), a seminal work on Islamic civilization.
bio_en:
Abu al-Hasan Ali al-Hasani an-Nadwi (1914-1999 CE / 1332-1420 AH) was one of the most prominent Indian Islamic scholars and thinkers of the 20th century. Born in Rai Bareli, Uttar Pradesh, into a family of scholars descended from the Prophet Muhammad, he studied at Nadwat al-Ulama in Lucknow and other institutions.

His most influential work, 'Madha Khasira al-Alam bi Inhitat al-Muslimin' (What the World Lost by the Decline of the Muslims), written in Arabic, argued that the decline of Islamic civilization adversely affected all of humanity. The book was endorsed by major scholars worldwide and translated into numerous languages. It remains a foundational text in modern Islamic thought.

An-Nadwi served as the rector of Nadwat al-Ulama, was a founding member of the Muslim World League, and served as chairman of the Oxford Centre for Islamic Studies. He authored over 200 books in Arabic and Urdu on Islamic history, biography, thought, and literature. He was known for his ability to communicate Islamic ideas effectively in Arabic to the Arab world while also being deeply rooted in the Indian scholarly tradition.

title: Sheikh
bio_short_en: Syrian Shafi'i scholar and professor at Damascus University, known for his accessible fiqh textbooks and scholarly editions of classical hadith works.
bio_en:
Mustafa Dib al-Bugha (1938-2020 CE / 1357-1441 AH) was a Syrian Shafi'i scholar and professor of Islamic jurisprudence at the University of Damascus. Born in Damascus, he studied under leading Syrian scholars and earned advanced degrees in Islamic law.

He is best known for his widely used fiqh textbook 'al-Fiqh al-Manhaji ala Madhab al-Imam ash-Shafi'i' (Systematic Jurisprudence according to the Shafi'i School), co-authored with Mustafa al-Khin and Ali ash-Shurbaji. This multi-volume work presents Shafi'i fiqh in a modern, accessible format and has been adopted in Islamic educational institutions worldwide.

Al-Bugha also produced important scholarly editions of classical texts, including a widely used annotated edition of Sahih al-Bukhari. His approach to presenting traditional Islamic knowledge in a format suitable for modern students made his works particularly influential in Islamic education.

title: Sheikh
bio_short_en: Egyptian Islamic scholar and thinker, author of numerous influential books on Islamic ethics, Quran interpretation, and social reform.
bio_en:
Muhammad al-Ghazali Ahmad as-Saqqa (1917-1996 CE / 1335-1416 AH) was one of the most influential Egyptian Islamic scholars and public intellectuals of the 20th century. Born in Nakla al-Inab, Beheira Governorate, he studied at al-Azhar University and became one of its most prominent graduates.

He authored over 60 books on Islamic thought, many of which became bestsellers in the Arab world. Notable works include 'Fiqh as-Sirah' (Understanding the Prophetic Biography), 'Khuluq al-Muslim' (The Character of the Muslim), 'Jaddid Hayatak' (Renew Your Life), and 'Kayfa Nata'amal ma'a al-Quran' (How to Engage with the Quran). His writing style was accessible, passionate, and addressed contemporary issues facing Muslim societies.

Al-Ghazali was a member of the Muslim Brotherhood in his youth but later distanced himself from political Islam. He was known for his centrist positions, advocating for women's rights within an Islamic framework and opposing both extremism and Western cultural imperialism. He was a professor at universities in Algeria and Saudi Arabia and died during a visit to Saudi Arabia.

name_full_en: Abu al-Falah Abd al-Hayy ibn Ahmad ibn al-Imad al-Hanbali
bio_short_en: Hanbali scholar and author of 'Shadharat adh-Dhahab,' a major biographical history organized chronologically by year of death.
bio_en:
Abu al-Falah Abd al-Hayy ibn Ahmad ibn al-Imad al-Hanbali (1623-1679 CE / 1032-1089 AH) was a Hanbali scholar from Damascus known for his major biographical and historical work. Born in Damascus, he studied under local scholars and became a respected figure in the city's scholarly community.

His magnum opus, 'Shadharat adh-Dhahab fi Akhbar man Dhahab' (Golden Nuggets: Concerning the History of the Departed), is one of the most important Islamic biographical dictionaries. Organized chronologically by year of death, it covers notable figures from the first Islamic century through the author's own time. The work draws on dozens of earlier biographical sources and provides concise but informative entries on scholars, rulers, poets, and other notable figures.

'Shadharat adh-Dhahab' became an essential reference work for Islamic historians and biographers, valued for its comprehensive coverage and accessibility. It fills gaps left by earlier biographical dictionaries and provides a continuous historical narrative through the biographies of notable individuals.

name_full_en: Shams ad-Din Muhammad ibn Ahmad ibn Abd al-Hadi al-Maqdisi
title: Imam
bio_short_en: Hanbali hadith scholar and student of Ibn Taymiyyah who died young but left important works on hadith criticism and Hanbali jurisprudence.
bio_en:
Shams ad-Din Muhammad ibn Ahmad ibn Abd al-Hadi al-Maqdisi (1305-1343 CE / 704-744 AH) was a Hanbali hadith scholar from the prominent Maqdisi family of Damascus and one of the students of Ibn Taymiyyah. Despite dying at approximately 38 years of age, he left a substantial scholarly legacy.

His works on hadith criticism include 'as-Sarim al-Munki fi ar-Radd ala as-Subki' (The Piercing Sword in Refutation of as-Subki), a defense of Ibn Taymiyyah's positions on visiting graves. He also authored works on hadith evaluation and biographical criticism of narrators.

Ibn Abd al-Hadi was known for his sharp mind and strong memory. Adh-Dhahabi, a contemporary, praised his intellectual abilities highly. His works in hadith sciences contribute to the Hanbali school's tradition of rigorous hadith-based scholarship, continuing the approach of his teacher Ibn Taymiyyah.

name_full_en: Diya' ad-Din Abu Abdullah Muhammad ibn Abd al-Wahid al-Maqdisi
title: Imam
bio_short_en: Hanbali hadith scholar from the Maqdisi family of Damascus, compiler of 'al-Ahadith al-Mukhtarah,' a collection of authentic hadiths selected through rigorous criteria.
bio_en:
Diya' ad-Din Abu Abdullah Muhammad ibn Abd al-Wahid al-Maqdisi (1173-1245 CE / 569-643 AH) was a Hanbali hadith scholar from the renowned Maqdisi family of Damascus. He founded the Dar al-Hadith ad-Diya'iyyah in Damascus, one of the important centers for hadith learning.

His most significant work is 'al-Ahadith al-Mukhtarah' (The Selected Hadiths), a collection of hadiths that he selected based on strict criteria of authenticity, though less stringent than the criteria of Bukhari and Muslim. The work aimed to gather sound hadiths not found in the two Sahihs, providing scholars with a reliable additional source of authenticated narrations.

Diya' ad-Din traveled extensively in pursuit of hadith, studying in Baghdad, Isfahan, Herat, and other centers of learning. He was known for his meticulous approach to hadith evaluation and his careful verification of chains of narration. His family, the Maqdisis of Damascus, produced many generations of Hanbali scholars.

name_full_en: Muhammad ibn Ali ibn Muhammad ibn Abdullah ash-Shawkani
title: Imam
bio_short_en: Yemeni scholar and advocate of ijtihad, author of 'Nayl al-Awtar,' a masterful commentary on hadiths of legal rulings, and 'Fath al-Qadir' in tafsir.
bio_en:
Muhammad ibn Ali ash-Shawkani (1759-1839 CE / 1173-1255 AH) was one of the most important Islamic scholars of the 18th-19th centuries. Born in Shawkan, Yemen, he became the chief judge (qadi al-qudat) of San'a and one of the most prolific and independent-minded scholars of his era.

His 'Nayl al-Awtar min Ahadith Sayyid al-Akhyar Sharh Muntaqa al-Akhbar' (The Attainment of the Objective: Commentary on the Select Narrations) is one of the most important works of hadith-based comparative fiqh, extracting legal rulings from prophetic narrations while presenting the positions of all schools. His Quran commentary 'Fath al-Qadir al-Jami' bayna Fannay ar-Riwayah wad-Dirayah min Ilm at-Tafsir' combines narration-based and reasoning-based exegesis.

Ash-Shawkani was a strong advocate of ijtihad (independent legal reasoning) and opposed the restriction of legal thought to a single madhab. His 'Irshad al-Fuhul ila Tahqiq al-Haqq min Ilm al-Usul' is a major work on principles of Islamic jurisprudence. Though born into a Zaydi context, he moved toward Sunni positions and his works are studied across all Sunni schools.

name_full_en: Abu al-Ala Muhammad Abdurrahman ibn Abdurrahim al-Mubarakfuri
title: Sheikh
bio_short_en: Indian hadith scholar, author of 'Tuhfat al-Ahwadhi,' the most comprehensive commentary on Jami at-Tirmidhi.
bio_en:
Abu al-Ala Muhammad Abdurrahman ibn Abdurrahim al-Mubarakfuri (1860-1935 CE / 1283-1353 AH) was an Indian hadith scholar from Mubarakpur, Uttar Pradesh. He is best known for his magnum opus 'Tuhfat al-Ahwadhi bi Sharh Jami at-Tirmidhi' (The Gift of the Resolute: Commentary on Jami at-Tirmidhi).

'Tuhfat al-Ahwadhi' is the most comprehensive and detailed commentary on Imam at-Tirmidhi's hadith collection. For each hadith, al-Mubarakfuri provides the chain analysis, explains difficult vocabulary, extracts legal rulings, reconciles apparent contradictions, and cites the positions of the major scholars. The work has become an essential reference for scholars and students of hadith worldwide.

He was a scholar of the Ahl-e-Hadith movement in India, emphasizing direct engagement with hadith texts and independent legal reasoning. His commentary on at-Tirmidhi demonstrates an encyclopedic knowledge of the hadith literature and the various schools of Islamic jurisprudence.

name_full_en: Abd al-Hayy ibn Fakhr ad-Din al-Hasani an-Nadwi
title: Sheikh
bio_short_en: Indian scholar and author of 'Nuzhat al-Khawatir,' an eight-volume biographical dictionary of Islamic scholars in the Indian subcontinent.
bio_en:
Abd al-Hayy ibn Fakhr ad-Din al-Hasani an-Nadwi (1869-1923 CE / 1286-1341 AH) was an Indian Islamic scholar and historian, and the father of Abu al-Hasan an-Nadwi. Born in Lucknow into a family of scholars tracing their lineage to the Prophet Muhammad, he studied under the scholars of Lucknow and Bhopal.

His monumental work, 'Nuzhat al-Khawatir wa Bahjat al-Masami' wan-Nawazir' (The Delight of Thoughts and the Joy of Listeners and Observers), is an eight-volume biographical dictionary documenting the scholars of the Indian subcontinent from the earliest period of Islamic presence in India up to his own time. The work, written in Arabic, covers thousands of scholars and is the most comprehensive reference for the intellectual history of Islam in South Asia.

Al-Hasani was also a teacher at Nadwat al-Ulama and authored other works on Islamic history. His biographical dictionary provides an irreplaceable record of the vast scholarly tradition of the Indian subcontinent.

title: Sheikh
bio_short_en: Palestinian Shafi'i judge and prolific author of works defending traditional Islamic scholarship and collecting prophetic praises.
bio_en:
Yusuf ibn Isma'il an-Nabhani (1849-1932 CE / 1265-1350 AH) was a Palestinian Islamic scholar, Shafi'i judge, and prolific author. Born in the village of Ijzim near Haifa, Palestine, he studied in Egypt at al-Azhar and served as a judge in the Ottoman court system across the Levant.

An-Nabhani is best known for his works in defense of traditional Sunni scholarship and his collections of poetry praising the Prophet Muhammad. His 'al-Anwar al-Muhammadiyyah min al-Mawahib al-Ladunniyyah' is a popular abridgement of al-Qastallani's work on the Prophetic biography. He compiled numerous collections of devotional poetry and litanies.

He was also a vigorous polemicist who wrote in defense of the Ash'ari creed, the validity of following a madhab, and the legitimacy of Sufi practices such as visiting the graves of saints. He served as a judge in Beirut and other cities during the final decades of the Ottoman Empire. His works represent the traditional Ottoman-era approach to Islamic scholarship.

name_full_en: Muhammad Rashid ibn Ali Rida al-Husayni at-Tarabulsi
bio_short_en: Syrian-Egyptian Islamic reformer, student of Muhammad Abduh, and author of 'Tafsir al-Manar,' an influential rationalist Quran commentary.
bio_en:
Muhammad Rashid ibn Ali Rida (1865-1935 CE / 1282-1354 AH) was a Syrian Islamic scholar, reformer, and public intellectual who became one of the most influential Muslim thinkers of the early 20th century. Born in Qalamoun near Tripoli, Lebanon, he emigrated to Egypt in 1897 and became the most prominent student and intellectual heir of Muhammad Abduh.

He founded the journal 'al-Manar' (The Lighthouse), which became the most influential Islamic periodical of its era, read across the Muslim world. He began the Quran commentary 'Tafsir al-Manar,' building on Abduh's lectures. The tafsir takes a rationalist approach, emphasizing the Quran's relevance to modern life and the need for Muslims to engage with contemporary knowledge.

Rashid Rida advocated for Islamic unity, the revival of the caliphate after its abolition in 1924, and a return to the Quran and Sunnah as the primary sources of Islamic law. His thought evolved over time, moving from the liberal reformism of Muhammad Abduh toward more conservative positions influenced by Salafi thought.

name_full_en: Muhammad at-Tahir ibn Muhammad ibn Muhammad at-Tahir ibn Ashur
title: Sheikh
bio_short_en: Tunisian Maliki scholar and Grand Mufti, author of 'at-Tahrir wat-Tanwir,' a major modern Quran commentary, and pioneering work on maqasid ash-shari'ah.
bio_en:
Muhammad at-Tahir ibn Ashur (1879-1973 CE / 1296-1393 AH) was a Tunisian Maliki scholar, Grand Mufti of Tunisia, and one of the most important Islamic thinkers of the 20th century. Born into the illustrious Ibn Ashur scholarly family, he studied at the Zaytuna Mosque-University and rose to become its rector and the Grand Mufti of the Maliki school in Tunisia.

His Quran commentary 'at-Tahrir wat-Tanwir' (The Liberation and Enlightenment) is considered one of the greatest tafsirs of the modern era. Spanning over 30 volumes, it combines traditional linguistic and legal exegesis with insights on the social, ethical, and civilizational dimensions of the Quran. His other major work, 'Maqasid ash-Shari'ah al-Islamiyyah' (The Objectives of Islamic Law), is a pioneering modern treatise on the higher objectives of Islamic legislation that significantly advanced the field of maqasid theory beyond ash-Shatibi's earlier work.

Ibn Ashur was a Maliki jurist and Ash'ari theologian who advocated for educational reform and the modernization of Islamic scholarship while maintaining deep commitment to traditional learning. His influence extends across the Arab and Islamic world.

title: Sheikh
bio_short_en: Egyptian scholar and historian, author of popular works on Islamic history, jurisprudence, and the history of Islamic legislation.
bio_en:
Muhammad ibn Afifi al-Bajuri, known as al-Khudari Bek (1872-1927 CE / 1289-1345 AH), was an Egyptian Islamic scholar, historian, and educator. He studied at al-Azhar and later at the Egyptian School of Law, combining traditional Islamic education with modern academic methods.

His most widely read works include 'Tarikh at-Tashri al-Islami' (The History of Islamic Legislation), which traces the development of Islamic jurisprudence from the prophetic era through the modern period, and 'Nur al-Yaqin fi Sirat Sayyid al-Mursalin' (The Light of Certainty in the Biography of the Chief of Messengers), a popular and accessible prophetic biography. He also authored 'Itmam al-Wafa fi Sirat al-Khulafa' on the lives of the Rightly Guided Caliphs.

Al-Khudari's works are known for their clarity and accessibility, making complex topics in Islamic history and law understandable to general readers. His books continue to be widely used as introductory texts in Islamic studies programs.

name_full_en: Badr ad-Din Abu Abdullah Muhammad ibn Ibrahim ibn Sa'd Allah ibn Jama'ah
title: Qadi
bio_short_en: Chief judge of Egypt and author of 'Tadhkirat as-Sami wal-Mutakallim,' a classic text on the etiquette of learning and teaching.
bio_en:
Badr ad-Din Muhammad ibn Ibrahim ibn Jama'ah (1241-1333 CE / 639-733 AH) was a prominent Shafi'i jurist who served as the chief judge (qadi al-qudat) of Egypt and Syria multiple times during the Mamluk period. Born in Hama, Syria, he studied in Damascus and Cairo under the leading scholars of his time.

He is best known for 'Tadhkirat as-Sami' wal-Mutakallim fi Adab al-'Alim wal-Muta'allim' (A Reminder to the Listener and Speaker on the Etiquette of the Scholar and Student), a comprehensive guide to the manners and ethics of Islamic education. The work covers the conduct of teachers, the behavior of students, the management of study circles, and the proper methods of seeking and transmitting knowledge.

Ibn Jama'ah also wrote on Islamic political theory and governance. His 'Tahrir al-Ahkam fi Tadbir Ahl al-Islam' discusses the duties of rulers and the Islamic political system. He was a scholar of great influence during the Mamluk period, combining deep learning with practical experience in the judiciary.

name_full_en: Sharaf ad-Din Muhammad ibn Sa'id al-Busiri
bio_short_en: Egyptian poet and author of 'Qasidah al-Burdah' (The Poem of the Cloak), the most famous and widely recited praise poem for the Prophet Muhammad.
bio_en:
Sharaf ad-Din Muhammad ibn Sa'id al-Busiri (1211-1294 CE / 608-694 AH) was an Egyptian poet of Moroccan Berber origin who composed the most celebrated praise poem for the Prophet Muhammad in Islamic literary history. Born in Delas, Upper Egypt, he studied under the Shadhili Sufi master Abu al-Abbas al-Mursi in Alexandria.

His 'al-Kawakib ad-Durriyyah fi Madh Khayr al-Bariyyah,' commonly known as 'Qasidah al-Burdah' (The Poem of the Cloak), is the most widely recited, memorized, and commented upon poem in Islamic civilization after the Quran. The 160-verse ode praises the Prophet's character, miracles, and spiritual station with extraordinary eloquence. According to tradition, al-Busiri composed it while suffering from paralysis and was healed in a dream when the Prophet placed his cloak (burda) upon him.

The Burdah has been translated into virtually every language spoken by Muslims, has attracted over 100 commentaries, and is recited in religious gatherings across the Muslim world. He also composed 'al-Hamziyyah,' another major poem in praise of the Prophet. Al-Busiri's poetry represents the pinnacle of the Islamic tradition of prophetic praise poetry.

title: Sheikh
bio_short_en: Syrian hadith scholar of Albanian origin, renowned for his critical editions of classical Islamic texts and hadith verification.
bio_en:
Abd al-Qadir ibn Muhammad al-Arna'ut (1928-2004 CE / 1347-1425 AH) was a Syrian hadith scholar of Albanian origin who became one of the most important editors and verifiers of classical Islamic texts in the 20th century. Born in Damascus into a family of Albanian descent, he studied under major Syrian scholars including Badr ad-Din al-Hasani.

Al-Arna'ut dedicated his career to producing critical scholarly editions of classical Islamic works, meticulously verifying hadith narrations and correcting scribal errors in manuscripts. His editions of important works include books by Ibn al-Qayyim, Ibn Rajab, and other major scholars. He worked closely with Shu'ayb al-Arna'ut (to whom he was not related by blood) in this endeavor.

He was known for his balanced approach to hadith grading, neither excessively strict nor lenient. His work contributed significantly to making classical Islamic texts available in reliable, accessible modern editions for scholars and students.

title: Sheikh
bio_short_en: Kurdish-Qatari scholar and Secretary General of the International Union of Muslim Scholars, specialist in Islamic economics and finance.
bio_en:
Ali Muhyi ad-Din al-Qaradaghi (b. 1949 CE / 1369 AH) is a Kurdish Islamic scholar from Qaradagh in Iraqi Kurdistan who has become one of the most prominent scholars of Islamic economics and finance. He serves as the Secretary General of the International Union of Muslim Scholars.

Al-Qaradaghi earned his doctorate from al-Azhar University and has served as a professor at the University of Qatar. He has authored extensively on Islamic finance, banking, and economics, contributing to the theoretical framework that underpins the modern Islamic finance industry. His works address contemporary financial instruments, insurance (takaful), and the adaptation of traditional Islamic commercial law to modern economic systems.

He is active in international Islamic scholarly organizations and has contributed to numerous fatwas and resolutions on contemporary issues. His expertise bridges traditional Islamic jurisprudence and modern economic theory.

bio_short_en: Indian Muslim academic and author of 'Hadith Literature,' an important English-language study of the history and methodology of hadith sciences.
bio_en:
Muhammad Zubayr Siddiqi (1904-1977 CE / 1322-1397 AH) was an Indian Muslim scholar and academic who served as a professor at Calcutta University. He is best known in the English-speaking world for his seminal work 'Hadith Literature: Its Origin, Development and Special Features.'

This book provides a comprehensive scholarly introduction to the history, development, and methodology of hadith literature for English-speaking readers. It traces the transmission of hadith from the prophetic era through the compilation of the major collections, explaining the methods used by hadith scholars to evaluate narrations. The work has been widely used as a textbook in Islamic studies programs in Western universities.

Siddiqi's contribution lies in making the complex Islamic science of hadith accessible to an international academic audience while maintaining scholarly rigor. His work helped bridge the gap between traditional Islamic hadith scholarship and modern Western academic study of Islamic sources.

title: Sheikh
bio_short_en: Egyptian Azhari scholar and author of 'Fath al-Mun'im,' a modern commentary on Sahih Muslim.
bio_en:
Musa Shahin Lashin (1920-2009 CE / 1338-1430 AH) was an Egyptian scholar and professor at al-Azhar University, specializing in hadith sciences. He authored 'Fath al-Mun'im Sharh Sahih Muslim' (The Opening of the Bestower: Commentary on Sahih Muslim), a comprehensive modern commentary on Imam Muslim's hadith collection.

This commentary is valued for its accessibility and its engagement with modern scholarly questions. For each hadith, Lashin provides linguistic explanations, legal rulings, and practical applications while drawing on classical commentaries and adding contemporary insights. The work fills an important gap in modern hadith scholarship, as most classical commentaries on Sahih Muslim are centuries old.

Lashin served on the faculty of al-Azhar University for decades and was respected for his deep knowledge of the hadith sciences and his ability to present classical scholarship in a modern academic framework.

name_full_en: Abdul-Fattah ibn Muhammad ibn Bashir Abu Ghuddah al-Halabi
title: Sheikh
bio_short_en: Syrian Hanafi hadith scholar and editor, known for his critical editions of classical hadith texts and his advocacy for traditional scholarship.
bio_en:
Abdul-Fattah ibn Muhammad Abu Ghuddah (1917-1997 CE / 1336-1417 AH) was a Syrian Hanafi hadith scholar from Aleppo who became one of the most influential Islamic scholars and editors of the 20th century. He studied under scholars in Aleppo and Egypt, including al-Kawthari, and became a leading voice for traditional Hanafi scholarship.

Abu Ghuddah dedicated his life to editing, annotating, and publishing classical Islamic texts, particularly in the hadith sciences. He produced acclaimed editions of works by al-Lucknawi, al-Kawthari, and other scholars. His notes and introductions to these works are themselves significant scholarly contributions. He also authored original works on hadith methodology and Islamic education.

He was a student and close associate of Muhammad Zahid al-Kawthari and continued al-Kawthari's legacy of defending traditional Hanafi-Maturidi scholarship. He taught in Syria, Saudi Arabia, and Turkey, and trained the next generation of hadith scholars, including Muhammad Awwamah. He was known for his meticulous attention to textual accuracy and his encyclopedic knowledge of the Islamic manuscript tradition.

title: Sheikh
bio_short_en: Syrian scholar from Aleppo, author of works on the attributes of the Prophet Muhammad and hadith sciences.
bio_en:
Muhammad Abdullah Siraj ad-Din al-Husayni (1924-2002 CE / 1342-1422 AH) was a Syrian Islamic scholar from Aleppo who was renowned for his deep devotion to the prophetic tradition and his scholarly works on the characteristics and attributes of the Prophet Muhammad.

His best-known works include 'Sayyiduna Muhammad Rasulullah' (Our Master Muhammad, the Messenger of God), a comprehensive study of the Prophet's attributes, characteristics, and miracles, and 'al-Salah ala an-Nabi' (Blessings upon the Prophet), a detailed treatment of the practice and virtues of sending prayers upon the Prophet. He also wrote on various aspects of hadith sciences and Islamic spirituality.

Siraj ad-Din was a descendant of the Prophet Muhammad through the Husayni lineage and was deeply embedded in the traditional scholarly culture of Aleppo. He was a teacher and mentor to many scholars and was known for his profound love for the Prophet, which permeated all his writings and teachings.

title: Sheikh
bio_short_en: Syrian Shafi'i scholar and professor at Damascus University, co-author of 'al-Fiqh al-Manhaji' and specialist in usul al-fiqh.
bio_en:
Mustafa Sa'id al-Khin (1927-2008 CE / 1346-1429 AH) was a Syrian Shafi'i scholar and professor of Islamic jurisprudence at the University of Damascus. Born in Damascus, he studied under the scholars of the city and at al-Azhar University before returning to teach at Damascus University.

He is best known as the co-author (with Mustafa al-Bugha and Ali ash-Shurbaji) of 'al-Fiqh al-Manhaji ala Madhab al-Imam ash-Shafi'i' (Systematic Jurisprudence according to the Shafi'i School), a multi-volume textbook that became one of the most widely used modern presentations of Shafi'i fiqh. He also authored works on usul al-fiqh (Islamic legal theory) and hadith sciences.

Al-Khin specialized in the principles of Islamic jurisprudence and made the field accessible to modern students through clear, systematic writing. His scholarly output, together with that of his colleagues at Damascus University, represented a golden era of traditional Shafi'i scholarship in modern Syria.

bio_short_en: Egyptian Islamic scholar and author of popular, accessible books on fiqh, seerah, and Islamic behavior widely used across the Arab world.
bio_en:
Hasan Muhammad Ayyub (1932-2008 CE / 1351-1429 AH) was an Egyptian Islamic scholar and prolific author who specialized in presenting Islamic knowledge in an accessible format for the general Muslim public. He studied at al-Azhar University and taught Islamic studies.

His works are known for their clarity and practical orientation. Notable titles include 'Fiqh al-Ibadat' (Jurisprudence of Worship), a popular manual covering the fundamentals of Islamic worship; 'as-Suluk al-Ijtima'i fi al-Islam' (Social Behavior in Islam); and biographical works on the prophets and the companions. His books have been widely read across the Arab world and translated into multiple languages.

Ayyub's contribution lies in making traditional Islamic scholarship accessible to ordinary Muslims. His clear writing style and practical approach to Islamic law and ethics made his books popular among a wide readership beyond the traditional scholarly class. His works continue to serve as introductory texts for those seeking to learn about Islamic practice and history.
